Success isn't from knowing everything.

It's from admitting you don't.

Most people are terrified of looking stupid:
• They won't ask "dumb" questions in meetings
• They nod along pretending to understand
• They stay quiet when confused

But the most successful people have no problem looking like idiots.
For a long long time.

When they want to learn something, they ask others or teach themselves:

Look around:

✅ Elon taught himself how to build rockets
✅ Zuckerberg learned coding alone
✅ Jobs studied calligraphy for fun

They all had one thing in common:
They became masters of self-education.

Here's how to start teaching yourself:

1/ Pick one thing at a time.

Don’t spread yourself too thin - you won’t make an impact. Focus on learning one thing at a time.

2/ Learn by doing.

Reading alone isn’t learning. 
Start a small project - learn what you need as you go.

3/ Learn from someone 2 steps ahead.

Learn from their mistakes - reverse engineer their success. 
Take notes on what they do differently.

The difference between winners and the rest?
It's how fast you learn and grow. 

Stop asking for permission.
It’s yours to take.

The most important thing I wish I started building sooner?
My personal brand.

📌 Here’s the playbook that got me to >450k followers
With 120 post ideas + my AI writing prompts: https://lnkd.in/gKzZUq-b

Remember:
The most important teacher you'll ever have? It's you.

♻ Repost to help your network teach themselves.

As a kid, my parents didn't have a lot of extra money for vacations so we roadtripped to lots of national parks. 

Of course, I didn't appreciate it and wanted to know why we couldn't go to Hawaii like all my friends. (what a brat I was).

Now, I love the outdoors and I have my Dad to thank for that. He passed 8 years ago and I think about him the most when I'm in nature. 

I hope to instill the same love of adventure my husband and I have for the mountains into our kids. 

This Memorial Day we took them up to the high Sierras for four days of camping and hiking. 

Proud mom moment -- my 4 YO daughter did a four mile hike, at 8,000 ft of elevation, with 1,000 ft of elevation gain (we went UP!). All by herself. 

My son also was a little champ, he so badly wants to copy his sister that he was determined to do his fair share of hiking too. 

It's easy to get caught up in the daily grind of a messy kitchen, slack requests, and long work hours. I'm so grateful for these breaks to recharge. 

Happy Memorial Day.

Domain-specific AI assurance is finally being taken seriously. But most reports still look too green, and that is a problem nobody wants to talk about.

Spent the afternoon at the annual Global AI Assurance Sandbox - matching AI vendors with assurance providers to run real-world governance and risk evaluations on practical use cases.

What was shown:

✅ High-risk use cases: HR matching, financial accounting, legal search, career guidance, and more. All live or near-production AI systems, tested against governance criteria by independent assurers.

✅ Most evaluations emphasized the need for domain-specific testing rather than generic benchmarks. This matches exactly what we see in industry demand.

Putting on my critique hat, here is what I think is important and want to see more going forward:

💡More work on risk-control mapping. Picking X evaluations is great, but the reality is industry struggles to know WHAT to evaluate for a specific use case in the first place.

💡 More work on interpretation. Lifting low-level results into risk ratings - which result is critical and blocking, whine one is high-risk and requires immediate mitigations. Assigning these ratings is non-trivial. It requires deeper context on the specific use case and deployment.

💡 Too many "green" results. We need to be honest and surface/share critical findings that require immediate mitigation. Transparency from assurance vendors on this is key. At my previous company, where we did smart contract audits, we routinely flagged critical (but mitigated) issues and added them in public reports to buid trust with users. Showing what was mitigated is good, noone expects that a use case is fully green from day one. That is how you build trust with users.

Great to see so many vendors pushing the frontier forward. The foundation is strong. Now it is about going one level deeper together.

LatticeFlow AI   
Asia Tech x Singapore

This weekend, I wrapped up our fiscal year and found myself looking back at the past 9 months.

When we first started, there were so many unknowns. 
How do we find our first customer? 
How do we build the product? 
Once we find customers, how do we make sure they're actually happy? 
How should the product even be shaped? 
We tried different approaches, different ideas, iterated constantly.

Looking back, I kept thinking about a Rumi quote: 
"As you start to walk on the way, the way appears. Clarity doesn't come before action. It comes from action."

One thing became crystal clear: in the beginning, you're going to do things that don't work out. So it's better to move fast, learn quickly, and move on. Whether it's your ICP, pricing strategy, marketing, or outbound, you only know what works when you start doing it.

I've spent 12 years reading entrepreneurship books, listening to countless founder podcasts. But the real learnings? They come from doing. Not from consuming.

The doubts, the questions, the things that feel like they matter so much early on, most of them don't. What sticks is focus. Staying true to what you're building. Leaving ego behind. Being ready to unlearn what you thought you knew.

The old ways might not work. New ways will emerge. But only if you keep walking.

Each challenge taught me something new. And honestly, that's what I've come to love most about this founder journey. 
It's not just about what you're building. It's about who you're becoming.

Here's to the next fiscal year. May we have the strength to keep building something incredible.

Essential books for product builders

I've put together a collection of my all-time favorite books, organized by their jobs-to-be-done. When your manager tells you to work on a particular development area—or if you’re just feeling the itch for self-improvement—these are the books to read.

To keep this list extremely high signal-to-noise, I forced myself to pick only three books per category (so hard!), and only books I’ve completed.

The collection includes both classics and under-the-radar gems. I very much like Marc Andreeson's take that you should mostly read books that are over 10 years old (because those are the books that have stood the test of time), so you’ll notice no super new books.

There are so many great titles that I didn’t include here, either because I haven’t had a chance to read them or they just didn’t make the cut. I’m sorry if I didn’t include your book, or a book you love. I probably forgot some important ones, too. That’s why we’ll have a part 2, coming soon!

Here's the collection: https://lnkd.in/gY6nA9A2

P.S. What a book I missed that legitimately made you a better builder/leader? Let me know in the comments. 

P.P.S. If you’re feeling like you have no time to read, I was in the same boat, especially after having a kid. Bryan Johnson's suggestion of reading a book for 10 minutes before bed changed my life. I started reading more books, and I got better sleep! Try it out.

There are 3 levels of using Claude.

Getting to advanced it easier than you think.

I run most of my company on Claude.

Most people stay stuck in beginner mode because nobody shows them what comes next.

Here are the 3 levels:

→ Level 1: Beginner 

Goal: Claude knows what you want with little context.

- Set your default tone to Normal, Concise, or Formal.
- Turn on memory so Claude can build context over time.
- Add user preferences for what to do and what to avoid.
- Choose the right model for each task.
- Use web search when the answer needs current information.
- Switch on code execution for calculations, charts, and file creation.

At this level, Claude gives you cleaner answers. But you're still prompting from scratch every time.

→ Level 2: Intermediate 

Goal: Stop re-explaining yourself.

- Create one project for each recurring task.
- Add project instructions ONCE so Claude works the way you want every time.
- Connect the apps you use daily like Gmail, Drive, Slack, or Notion.
- Turn repeated prompts into reusable Skills.
- Update memory as your preferences and responsibilities change.

At this level, Claude actually starts to remember you. You can stop explaining the same task every time you open a new chat.

→ Level 3: Advanced 

Goal: Turn repeated tasks into an operating system.

- Let Claude work directly in your local folders using Cowork.
- Schedule recurring tasks so they run without you starting them.
- Package your custom skills, connectors, and instructions into Plugins.
- Dispatch complex workflows from your phone to your desktop.
- Use the Chrome extension to automate recurring online tasks.

At this level, Claude runs full workflows with the context already built in.

After 6 months, Claude knows how I work better than most people I know 😅. 
And every session makes it a little better.

Successful 1-person businesses are more possible than ever. 

These 6 simple steps will help you build your own: 

If you're feeling stuck in the 9-5 routine, 
Or simply want more control over your future...

A 1-person business is the ideal route to take. 

It is a path to more financial and time freedom. 
But it will take work to get started. 

I've put together a 6-step roadmap to make it happen: 

1️⃣ Spot the rising tide early

You don't need a new idea.
You need to spot the tide that's already rising.

Ask yourself the questions laid out in the sheet below.

You can definitely use AI to your advantage here... ↓

2️⃣ Build for AI search and human trust at the same time

In 2026, discovery requires both AI answers and human feeds. 

What this actually means:

- Your website becomes a source AI can understand and cite
- Your personal brand becomes the trust layer that AI references
- Your content answers customer questions directly and clearly 

3️⃣ Build the simplest version that rides the tide

Build the smallest version that proves demand.

The strongest MVPs in 2026:

- Cohorts or memberships
- Newsletters with paid tiers
- Digital products
- Software or tools
- Templates or frameworks

The sheet below has more guidance on how to pressure test demand 👇 

4️⃣ Build your marketing funnel (go beyond just content)

Complex funnels are done. In 2026, there are only 3 stages: 

Awareness: Make sure the right people know you exist 
Consideration: Help people decide if you're right for them
Conversion: Transform trust into revenue 

5️⃣ Use AI to buy back time

Use it to:

✅ Draft, edit, and repurpose content
✅ Answer repeated customer questions
✅ Analyse what's working and what isn't
✅ Support onboarding, delivery, and support

Don't use it to:

❌ Replace judgment
❌ Hide weak thinking
❌ Scale an unproven model

6️⃣ Optimise based on your goals

A one-person business in 2026 isn't about doing "more". 

It's about creating a simpler, more fulfilling life. 

That means:

- Fewer products
- Fewer platforms
- Clear metrics that make sense
- Systems that compound quietly
- A business that works without constant input 

All 3 of my 8-figure businesses were built on these exact principles.

And now I teach others my exact playbook so they can do the same. 

Will you build your one-person business this year?
Comment below and I'll see if I can give some advice !

We generated ~6M LinkedIn impressions from executive content this past quarter. Here’s why Clay invested in this channel and how ultimately we think about it:

Problem we have to solve: 

As a marketing team, we support both our sales-led and self-serve motion. Because of that, we have multiple different types of personas that we need to serve. 

The problem is all of these core personas spend time on one channel, which is LinkedIn. What do you do when you need to increase awareness in other personas, but everyone still primarily consumes content in one channel? 

Hypothesis:

People want to learn from real operators. Leveraging the brand authority and knowledge of our internal executives will enable us to increase awareness with more vertical audiences. 

Implementation:

1. Map our core personas on Self-Serve and Sales-led.

Clay’s end user is go-to-market engineers, ops, and growth. For this persona we invested in Everett's (Head of GTM Engineer) and Davide’s (Head of Growth) profile.

Marketing and Revenue leaders are executive buyers in every deal. We invested in my profile, Varun, and Rob (Head of Sales Development). We also added Todd (Enterprise Sales) after he joined us, and Becca (Head of Sales) is going to ramp soon.

Varun and Kareem naturally attract the founder persona. 

Together, we’ve added 80k new followers so far.

2. Ok, but how do you get executives to actually post? 

Everyone knows that this is important, the difficult part is getting people to post. After trial and error, here’s the model that worked:

- It needs to be a core metric for awareness targets. Your team needs to be accountable for growing this surface area
- Bring someone to actually lead this function. In our case, we have the one and only Sarah leading this. 
- Leverage contractors like Alec and Good Content to scale this motion. 
- Build a weekly cadence for people to post. Weekly meetings and ongoing Slack messages to get posts out. 

3. Establish a high bar for helpful content. 

We don’t want anyone publishing slop. The good thing about having operators writing their own content is that they can always ask themselves - “am I actually learning anything from this?” If the answer is no, you should not post it. 

Learnings:

- You have to have this as a topline awareness goal to make it work. Otherwise, you won’t feel the need to execute this motion. 
- Getting executives to write will always be the bottleneck. You need to have someone who’s borderline annoying to ensure this moves forward. 
- Executives will understand the value once they start to see results. Things will feel smooth after that. 

Have any questions about LinkedIn + Exec Content?

AMA in the comments.

One founder I really admire is Rahul Vohra 

What I like most about him is that he didn’t invent a new category. He took one of the oldest products on the internet and made people care about it again.

He taught himself to code at age 8 to build games. And games teach you something most founders miss early: people remember how a product feels far longer than what it does.

Rahul carried that philosophy into Superhuman Mail.

Email is a free product that has existed since 1971 and somehow, he convinced people to pay $30/month for it.

The market didn’t need another email app. What it was missing was one built by someone who actually cared, obsessively and unreasonably, about details most founders never even notice.

→ How fast each screen and email opens
→ The timing of an animation
→ Whether a shortcut removes a single unnecessary click
→ Whether the interface feels good enough to want to come back to

He even had 20 full-time employees conducting personalized 30-minute onboarding calls for every new user, just to shape the experience correctly from day 1.

That philosophy built an $800M+ company, later acquired by Grammarly last year.

And the deeper insight behind all of it was simple:

Products win twice. First emotionally, then commercially.

Before people pay with money, they pay with attention, trust, and attachment.

Rahul proved that even the most boring category becomes valuable again when someone cares enough to perfect the experience.

I paid $1,500 to learn how to be exceptional.

It can be summed up in 4 words:

Being exceptional equals volume.

Two years ago, I paid $1,500 for a coaching session with someone who'd built one of the biggest audiences in my niche.

Which felt insane. I almost didn't book it.

The first thing they did was open a spreadsheet.

500 posts. Every metric tracked. Every topic color-coded. Every repost. Every format. Every visual type. Sorted and studied.

That's when I understood…the gap between average and exceptional isn't just 10% more effort.

It's more like 1,000%.

Average people wait for 1 good idea. Exceptional ship 1 bad idea to learn from it.
Average people quit after 3 failures. Exceptional improve through 300 failures.

After that call, I started tracking everything.

Every post. Every metric. Every viral piece of content I came across, saved and adapted with my own take.

It worked.

My businesses now do over $1m a year.
The gap between average and exceptional is always about volume.

You could fundamentally rebuild your marketing team around AI now, and it would look totally different from your current org chart. Different structure, skills, and ways of working.

I’m increasingly in the camp that you can’t just integrate AI into how a team works; you’ll need to fundamentally rethink how the team works, is structured, and what its core skills are.

This is my first draft of a v1 of what an agentic marketing org would look like.

Layer 1 is Context/Intelligence:- This is the biggest unlock you can implement right now. Every org can now have a shared intelligence layer. Pre-AI, all this intelligence lived in your employees' minds and left with them when they left. Now you can build a system that captures that intelligence and benefits the entire team. It consists of your market intelligence, how your team operates, and what’s working and what isn’t. It’s automatically updated from the work you do and from internal and external learning. Everything else reads from this and updates it with learnings.

Likely created by AI ops, enriched by marketing generalists/specialists.

Layer 2 is Execution:- Execution is going to be a mix of agents and true craftspeople. Why do I say ‘craftspeople’? Because being ‘good enough’ isn’t going to cut it. I believe AI can draft 70%-80% of something. To stand out, that last 20%-30% will make all the difference. True craftspeople who truly understand the customer and the discipline, and who learn quickly, will be difference-makers.

Agents can be created by a core AI ops function, which is revops from what I’m seeing in most orgs, but should also be created by marketers, with craftspeople enriching output e.g. the last 20%-30%.

Layer 3 is Orchestration. This, to me, will be one of the most important roles to fill. You’ll need someone to manage your org/system. It’s not like a traditional org, with tree structures connecting functional teams. Your org chart is a living, breathing system. You need to manage a system that’s producing outputs. Routing decisions, monitoring outputs, ensuring it’s running correctly, and ensuring agents read from the correct context.

Most companies are using revops or just an AI generalist to do this, from what I’ve seen.

Layer 4 is Leadership:- The new marketing leadership role is to understand how to create this system, refine it, and generate output from the system. Your job shifts from coordinating people to setting direction for a system.
The thing most people get wrong: they assume AI hollows out the middle of the org. I think it does the opposite. It removes the coordination tax and concentrates humans at the points where they actually add value.

All layers (2, 3, 4) feed into context/intelligence.

I put together a graphic showing how this works. It’s the first draft, but I think it’s close.

Great session at Google yesterday on how AI is disrupting the old disruptors, i.e. SaaS!
My "role" on the panel was to add perspective on what's actually being disrupted from our "AI-native" perspective at LF. My key points:

1. Focus where one needs a trusted service, not mere code & capabilities - critical services in finance, pharma, etc., won’t be replaced by vibe coding. Not because they're complex, models can handle that, but because they require reliability, trust, certifications. For these services, the sticky part is not the code, but the trust.

2. Build for agents, not humans - software today is designed for humans navigating fancy UIs with buttons and workflows. That's going away soon. Agents will consume your service - so design for APIs, structure so your service is actually used. So the wrong but often common direction of "adding" AI to your service is completely missing the point - invert it, don't add AI, make your service easy to consume by AI!

Our last release last year was built exactly with this vision in mind, and although it felt crazy back then, today positions us as the most "agent-ready" product in our space as we focused on clean API & structure, not UIs.

3. And finally, don't be afraid of disruption. It's a rolling game (or "infinite" in the words of Simon Sinek). Everyone gets disrupted. The goal is to push things forward!

Thanks Janaina Poeta Frey, Hans Tran, Swiss Startup Association for the invite, great discussion.

I looked everywhere for a comprehensive AEO/GEO guide.

I found nothing, which is great news for you...

And this might be the biggest opportunity in digital marketing right now. 

Before I get into it, we have one of our most valuable masterclasses coming up.

I spent 3 hours searching for authoritative AEO/GEO content. 
And found nothing that was truly valuable.

This is a rising tide moment, and as a result...
The guides that were accurate 3 months ago aren't really anymore.

So here's the most up-to-date process of how the content you create can be cited by AI.

Save this for when you're next optimising your website's content:

1️⃣ The user asks a question. 
↳ This is the prompt, typically longer than an SEO query.

2️⃣ ChatGPT detects intent.
↳ Are you researching, shopping, or just curious?

3️⃣ It refines your wording.
↳ Messy prompts get cleaned up into a structured query.

4️⃣ It locates sources.
↳ Two "retrieval agents" step in. 
↳ One scrapes the public web, the other from search and high-trust platforms.

5️⃣ It retrieves data.
↳ Blogs, news, social, forums on one side...
↳ Reddit, Google, YouTube, LinkedIn, Quora, etc on the other.

6️⃣ Data flows back.
↳ The model now has a pile of raw context to work with.

7️⃣ It builds the final reply. 
↳ Weighs the sources, picks who to cite, and drops the rest.

8️⃣ It checks the memory.
↳ Short term (this chat) and long term (everything it knows about the user).

9️⃣ It shows the result.
↳ A handful of brands will be named. Everyone else is invisible !

All of this happens in a matter of seconds.

Which, conceptually, is pretty crazy compared to navigating a sea of blue links...

And how can you influence this process?

✅ Get on the platforms AI pulls from, like Reddit, LinkedIn, etc.
✅ Structure your content for AI extraction (FAQ schema, for example).
✅ Get cited by sources AI already trusts (backlinks, third-party mentions, etc).

There are 2.5 billion prompts entered per day for ChatGPT.

Imagine how powerful that could be if you owned your niche?

This is exactly what we're breaking down in our upcoming masterclass.
You'll walk away knowing how to increase visibility across the top 4 AI models.

How are founders meant to know how to pitch to VCs if VCs don't explain how their decision making process works? 😠

Today I'm sharing our investment memo scoring matrix

Prior to our investment committee meeting, each section gets a score

The team reads the memo and we use the scores to focus the discussion

The scores are 1, 2a, 2b or 3

1 - world class
2a - very strong, but something missing
2b - acceptable, but some significant concerns
3 - not investable

As a founder, you can use this structure to do two things

Work through the areas to anticipate likely questions from investors

Ensure your pitch and materials address the topics as best you can

The aim isn't to have all 1s - no company is perfect and our job is taking risk - but it is to ensure you surface any weaker areas and have a clear narrative

At the end of the process, we share the score card and memo with the founders

Questions? Just let me know in the comments 👇

The teams that come to us usually say they need to build a Salesforce integration faster. 

By the second conversation, they’re usually talking about something else entirely. A security review they failed and don’t fully understand. 

A deployment that worked in sandbox and broke during the first production install. A packaging decision they made early that they now realize they can’t really undo. Speed is what they ask for. 

A few minutes later, the real problem starts showing up. I think I learned to notice that way earlier than Appnigma. Back when I worked at Sears, selling appliances, customers would walk in asking about refrigerator specs or price comparisons. A couple of questions later, they’d start talking about the kitchen renovation they’d been putting off for years or why the last appliance purchase went badly. You learn pretty quickly that the first question usually isn’t the full question. 

A lot of the conversations I had later at Salesforce felt similar. ISV teams would come in with a technical issue, but underneath it was usually a process problem or a decision they wished they’d made differently months earlier. What I didn’t fully realize until building Appnigma was how much of that knowledge only existed in conversations. 

The same explanations kept happening over and over, usually after a team had already run into the problem. That’s really what we’re building now. Not just faster generation, but the context behind the decisions people usually only understand after deployment.

Recently, I was at a CEO dinner in San Francisco. 12 tech CEO’s around the table.

 The hottest topic that evening was the future of CRM in an AI reality.

Not whether Salesforce and HubSpot are under pressure, that was a given. Every second post on LinkedIn spells the doom of those two. The question was: what comes next?

A CRM is fundamentally a system of record. 

You use it to document what happened, whether that’s a meeting, a call, or a deal. That's the core value proposition.

But if that documentation starts living inside an agent, what do you actually need the CRM for?

We swapped HubSpot for Attio six months ago, and it was like a straightjacket being removed from our GTM organisation. 

Rigid processes shifted to agility and the overall clock speed of our team went up.

So what is my take? 

Is the answer in the new wave of AI-native CRM like Attio, Reevo, or Aurasell AI?

If you are a fast growing/changing tech company, the playbook is no longer Hubspot, but any of the new, Ai-native CRMs. We made the switch and succeeded. 

Whether the whole market is moving toward AI-native CRMs is a whole different question.

I believe that the CRM concept will eventually become obsolete, when the entire funnel turns agentic. What comes next is still being defined.

It won't happen overnight, but the direction is clear.

Seize the day.

That's the biggest lesson from hitting 130% of my annual quota in 2025.

When I left Salesforce in April last year, everything was blurry. I talked to 15 people before landing our first customer.

Then came the brutal part: An entire month with zero customers. Nothing. Until the very last day, one finally came through.

I could have panicked.

Instead, I asked myself every single morning: "What will I do TODAY?"

Did I leave a comment on someone's post? 
Did I send that follow-up message? 
Did I reach out to one more person?

Because here's what I learned:
Every comment you leave starts a conversation. 
Every message you send keeps a door open. 
Every follow-up compounds into something bigger.

When I started, I wrote down my annual goals. Then I broke them down into monthly, weekly, daily goals.

Some weeks were strong. Some months felt impossible.
But I couldn't control the year. I could only control the day.

Seize the day, and the year takes care of itself.

That's it. That's the breakthrough.

Daily actions turned into conversations. 
Conversations turned into customers. 
Customers turned into 130% of quota.

If you're in your own dead month right now, here's what I know:
You can't seize the year without seizing the day.

So what will you do TODAY that moves you forward?

2026, let's go!

Every creative medium starts as slop.

New technology lowers the barrier to creation and mints a new creator class. At first, the new creators are mocked as amateurs. But then the tools improve and so does the work. From these communities rise powerful distribution platforms that reshape culture. 

Take YouTube as an example. It started with silly homemade videos like keyboard cat, Numa Numa, and Chocolate Rain. Then came Ryan Higa’s sketches, Justin Bieber’s discovery, and OK Go’s treadmill music video. Over time, quality rose, legitimacy followed, and YouTube became the world’s largest video platform.

Every technology shift mints a new creator class that the old guard mocks.

Bloggers weren’t “real writers”. 
Twitter microbloggers weren’t “real bloggers”. 
YouTubers weren’t “real filmmakers.” 
Instagram influencers weren’t “real tastemakers”. 
TikTok teens weren’t “real creators.”

Cast out by the old gatekeepers, they build a new home for their community, and in doing so, invent the next platform.

Is “AI slop” this generation’s cat videos? What critics dismiss as junk is often content that would’ve been prohibitively expensive or impossible to make without AI: podcasts with historical or fictional guests, Pokemon nature documentaries, cooking shows hosted by animals, romantasy microdramas, Ghibli in real life, Dramione fanvids. These technically imperfect but creatively unbounded experiments are just the first wave of a sea change in UGC content.

This new AI-native generation of creators — the Generators — conjure worlds instead of filming them, blending ideas, moodboards, scripts, and real footage with prompts and generative AI models. They create, remix, regenerate,and refine until it becomes something new. 

Today, many are teenagers in their bedrooms, not too different from how prior UGC revolutions began. Over time, this new creator class will become more mainstream: film students making dragons on dorm room budgets, camera-shy solopreneurs sharing their real voices and expertise through avatars, small businesses turning iPhone photos into cinematic ads, scriptwriters becoming their own showrunners, creative directors running one-person agencies. 

When new creators appear, old platforms rarely welcome them. YouTube and TikTok reward face-forward performance, not surrealist fantasy. A new home may rise for the Generators, with audiences who love their worlds and communities who nurture them. 

AI doesn’t just lower the cost of creation. It expands who gets to create.

Did you know that the scope of your ISMS and your certification scope don't have to be equal?

Many organisations that I run through a Stage 1 audit believe that their scope has to be equal to the scope statement on their desired certificate.

That's not necessary, for good reason.

ISMS scope: 
↳ The boundaries and applicability of your information security management system — the organisational, ICT, and physical perimeter within which your management system operates.

Certification scope:
↳ The portion of your ISMS that a certification body audits and lists on your ISO 27001 certificate.

They can be identical. Or the certificate can cover a deliberate subset.

—
I wrote a full breakdown of this in this week's GRC Lab newsletter. Link in the comments.

Everyone was like: I have more findings than I can read, more PRs than I can review, more alerts than I can investigate 🤯 . The actual bottleneck is choosing what to focus on and understanding why.

A CTO put it cleanly: the value is determining what to fix in the first place, because otherwise you spend all your time reviewing 50 pull requests for the same stack trace that you just trash.

A enterprise software scanning product vendor says his tool finds 2 million issues. Half a million are red. He asked me, on a call: so my customer now asks me what the heck do we do with all these now?

The AI tooling market spent a year building autonomous coders. Meanwhile, the bug resolution constraint moved upstream. It's not throughput. It's judgement 🧠 

Where does your team's triage workflow break for you?

There are two fundamentally different ways to bet on a company.

One is the spreadsheet exercise. Revenue quality, contract length, billing frequency, logo retention. Underwrite the numbers and let them tell the story.

The other is the founder bet. Pre-revenue, sometimes pre-product. You're betting on a person and a vision. That's how Apple, Facebook, Google, and most of what matters in AI today got funded.

Both are legitimate. But they're not the same thing. And confusing them is a mistake.

The spreadsheet exercise works when the market is known, the product is proven, and the risk is execution. 

The founder bet works when the market doesn't exist yet and the only thing that matters is whether this person can build something nobody else can see.

Silicon Valley built its entire identity on the second model. Europe still defaults to the first.

That gap explains a lot.

Last week, Jonathan Golden (Airbnb’s first PM and Founding Partner at Deviation Capital) came to our office for a fireside chat with the team.

Jonathan helped scale Airbnb 100x and is now an investor backing the next generation of category-defining companies including Kalshi, Delphi, Whoop, and more.

A lot of what Jonathan lived through at Airbnb maps directly to the problems we’re solving at Paraform today.

We’re uniquely building for companies, recruiters, and candidates at the same time. That means the hardest problems are rarely just product problems. They’re trust problems, liquidity problems, quality problems, and coordination problems.

A few things that felt especially relevant to us:

1. Liquidity has a magic number

Marketplaces don't get better just because there’s more supply. They get better when there’s enough of the right supply for demand to reliably convert.

For Airbnb, that magic number was enough reviewed listings in a market for guests to find the right place. For Paraform, it means the right recruiter and candidate density by niche, so when a company opens a critical role, companies are able to immediately connect with supply.

2. Trust is product infrastructure

Airbnb’s Host Guarantee was not just a PR response to a trust crisis. It became part of the product infrastructure that made strangers comfortable transacting with each other.

The same is true for us. Companies need to trust candidate quality and protection of their brand. Recruiters need to trust the role, feedback loop, and payouts. 

In marketplaces, trust is not a value you state or something you add retroactively, it’s THE product.

3. Great marketplaces absorb friction

Marketplaces work when they make messy transactions feel simple. Airbnb didn’t remove the uncertainty between guests and hosts. It absorbed it through product, policies, support, and guarantees.

That absorption isn’t always automations. Airbnb had support agents all over the world showing up door to door and got employees stay at new Airbnbs to manually leave reviews in new geographies without density.

We’re at a major inflection point as a company; on track to become one of the fastest marketplaces to $100M, expanding across new products and verticals, and stepping into what I believe will be the most defining chapter in Paraform’s history. 

The opportunity ahead of us is massive, and we’re lucky to have the guidance and perspective of people like Jonathan who've been there and done it.

The clearest explanation of why creator payments take 90 days that I've seen  popped up in my Threads (older post but you can tell what my interests are).

They weren't mad. They were explaining.

"Most deals will be managed by an agency, very few brands do their payments in house. So you go to invoice the agency to pay you out, the agency then has to go through their finance team to deal with the payment, that finance team request is two weeks long because they are handling all payments within the organization. On top of that, the brand needs to send the money over still, so the agency has to invoice THEM, and then same thing, they have to go through their finance department."

Their conclusion: "That one payment has now passed through 5 peoples hands before the money even starts moving."

That's the whole problem in one sentence.

It's not that anyone in the chain is incompetent. It's that the chain itself is the bug. Brand finance → agency AR → agency AP → creator. Four queues, two procurement systems, one creator who did the work eight weeks ago and is now wondering if they should mention it on their podcast.

Here's what most marketers don't realize: this isn't an agency problem to solve. The agency is also the victim of it. Their AP team is drowning in 1099s for the same reasons your brand's AP team is. They didn't build the broken pipe, they're just standing in it.

And here's the part that should bother every brand running this model. The creator doesn't blame the agency. They blame you. Your logo was in the brief. Your product was in the post. When they tell every other creator in their network that the payment took 90 days, they're not saying "Agency X was slow." They're saying "Brand Y was slow."

The fix isn't picking a faster agency. It's collapsing the chain. Fund the payment directly, route it through one vendor that handles onboarding, tax, and compliance, and let the creator get paid in days instead of quarters. The agency still runs the program. The brand still has the relationship. The five-person hand-off becomes one.

If your agency is telling you Net 90 is just how it works, they're describing their own back office, not the actual physics of moving money.

Monarchxr > thank you for laying it out. To every brand still routing creator payments through a four-team relay race: your creators are talking about you. The bar is embarrassingly low. Be the one who fixes the chain.

IRR is not a useful metric in venture until a fund is 4, 5, or 6 years old. 

Been reading a number of stories about how IRR for venture funds raised in the last 2 years has gotten out to "hot start" or is "outpacing older vintages".

...well of course it is. But who cares? 

IRR is not a good comparative measure early on specifically because it takes into account the value of time. So if a fresh fund makes an investment then has that portfolio company marked up in the span of a few months (not uncommon in this AI frenzy), bam, printing IRR. 

But look at 2021. Lots of funds started that year and saw explosive IRR growth out of the gate. And now...struggling. Holding lots of companies whose last major uptick was 3-4 years ago and whose stale marks are starting to drag on the same IRR metric that looked so rosy back when. 

Are we headed for the same future in this vintage? I believe far more in the AI transformation than in the NFT craze, but no doubt there are some wild overvaluations happening. 

History may not repeat, but it tends to rhyme.

65% of hedge funds now employ AI/ML strategies (Preqin). But after the tariff shocks of 2025, allocators are increasingly asking: can your AI model show its work?
The research case for ML in security pricing is well-established. Gu, Kelly, and Xiu alongisde @QuantumStreetAI’s own work demonstrated that advanced ML models more than double predictive accuracy relative to linear benchmarks when tested against unseen data.
But performance alone does not meet institutional requirements. The signal does not operate in isolation. Risk models, transaction cost constraints, turnover limits, and capital allocation rules all shape how a forecast becomes a position.
SHAP has emerged as the practical standard for return attribution, giving investment committees an auditable view into which signal combinations drove each decision. That is what separates ML strategies that meet governance requirements from those that remain research-grade.
At @QuantumStreetAI, explainability is built into the investment process. Every forecast is fully attributable through SHAP, decomposing each signal into its underlying drivers so portfolio managers can defend positions in economic terms, not model outputs.

I co-hosted a GTM ping pong tournament in SF last Friday with top CROs and sales leaders. Three patterns from the side conversations have stuck with me.

The CRO still initiates the AI buy. The CISO approves it. More of our Sybill deals now have CIO, CISO, and CTO showing up during scoping. Not as final decision-makers, but as validators. Bring them in too early and you're pitching everyone at once. Too late and they block you at the finish line. The signal you're in the window: scoping calls start including questions about data residency or SOC 2 scope before pricing comes up.

The # 1 objection from tech-forward teams: "We'll just pipe call recordings into an LLM." The logic sounds reasonable. After 500+ conversations with revenue leaders who tried it, the seams show fast. The moment you ask anything that requires understanding across your pipeline, raw transcripts dumped into an LLM behave like a search engine. An organized context graph plus an LLM behaves like an intelligence system. The companies that try to build internally almost always call us back within a quarter.

Top performers get efficiency. Median reps get capability. That distinction is the whole post. Top performers get faster follow-ups and shorter prep. Median reps can ask "how should I move this deal forward?" and get ideas that close the skill gap. One extra deal closed per rep is worth 5-10x what time savings delivers. Most of your pipeline runs through median reps.

The best podcast guests have two traits:

1. They have achieved immense amounts.  

2. They do not give a s*** what people think of them. 

The two greatest sales leaders I have ever met are Chris Degnan and Chad Peets. Without a doubt.  

Today, I sat down with both of them to learn what it takes to build a sales team in a world of AI. I condensed my notes below:

🚀 8 Lessons on Building a $4B+ Revenue Machine

1. Get Rid of Order Takers:

Do not be blinded by a resume with flashy, tier-one logos. If someone has been at Salesforce or ServiceNow for five years, they have likely never opened a new logo because they operate in a monopoly. Look for "grit" by hiring reps who succeeded at tier-three or mediocre companies where they actually had to generate their own pipeline.

2. Hire the "Athlete," Not the Industry Expert:

Hiring purely for industry background is a mistake. Industry expertise doesn't matter nearly as much as a rep's core DNA, determination, and the quality of the sales organization that trained them. 

3. Stop Tying Comp to "On-Demand" Revenue 

Paying your sales team on monthly in-arrears or usage billing with no commitment is naive and dangerous. Without a booked, annual contract, you have zero moat, no customer loyalty, and massive churn risk if a competitor drops a new feature tomorrow. Force friction into the sales cycle and pay only on booked contracts.

4. Guard Against the A-Player Exodus:

Setting quotas arbitrarily high just because you are an "AI company" will destroy your morale. If world-class reps stop making money, they will quit. When your A-players walk out the door, the rest of the market finds out, and you will inevitably be forced to replace an A-player sales org with B-players. 

5. Track the Direct Correlation Between Flights and Success:

A remote sales culture is a failing sales culture if it turns into a three-day workweek. Frontline and second-line managers need to get out of the office and onto sales calls. If you want to know if a sales manager is failing, pull up their T&E budget. If they haven't been on a plane in weeks, they aren't working.

6. Performance Management is a Quarterly Law, Not an Annual Event:

A healthy sales organization must actively attribute and cut the bottom 10% of its sales force every single year. This isn't an annual HR exercise; it has to be handled quarterly through brutal accountability. Good performers don't fear this; they prefer it because it proves their work is elite.

7. Beware of "Glorified" Forward Deployed Engineers:

The best engineers do not want to be FDEs. FDEs are a mask for an inferior product. It leaves the customer holding a bag of technical debt that never makes it back into the core product engine. Build a world-class core product first.

Watched CNN cover Anthropic delaying its latest model because of cybersecurity risk.
That is impressive. It is also a terrifying warning sign.
Because the message is clear. Models are getting so capable at cybersecurity tasks that the speed and scale of attacks can change fast. CNN quoted Anthropic’s Logan Graham saying, “If models are going to be this good … we need to prepare pretty fast.”
I think that is understated.
We are already in the era where AI coding tools do not just write code. They execute it. They touch your filesystem. They operate across tools, configs, permissions, and integrations.

So we did a simple test.
We ran LogicStar AI on the leaked Claude Code codebase. It found more than 10 real security issues within minutes.
Not theoretical. Not “prompt injection” demos. Real issues with real blast radius:
Headless mode enabling remote code execution without any prompt or warning
Claude MCP server allowing arbitrary file writes, including via an undocumented parameter
Permission model gaps allowing reads of sensitive files, including traversal bypasses via Grep and Glob despite deny rules
We responsibly disclosed the issues via HackerOne.
My takeaway is not “Claude is unsafe” or “models are bad.”
My takeaway is that we need a new default: Preventative Maintenance for Code.
Comment CODE and I will DM you a link to try LogicStar on your own project.
CNN Business Sean Lyngaas, Thomas Friedman

"𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝗴𝘂𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗼𝗿 𝗟𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿"

Monday, 9am. 𝗘𝗺𝗮𝗶𝗹 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗖𝗔:

"𝗥𝗲: 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗥𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 – 𝗔𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗨𝘀𝗮𝗴𝗲 𝗶𝗻 𝗕𝗮𝗻𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴

We are conducting a review of AI system governance at UK banks. Please provide within 30 days:
1. Complete inventory of AI systems in production
2. Risk assessments for each AI system
3. Evidence of bias testing and fairness measures
4. Model governance frameworks and documentation
5. Third-party AI vendor due diligence records"

𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝘁𝗼 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘁𝗲𝗮𝗺: "𝗗𝗼 𝘄𝗲 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗱𝗼𝗰𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻?"

𝗥𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗳𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸:
1. "We have some model documentation, but it's not centralized..."
2. "Bias testing? We asked vendors if they test for bias. They said yes..."
3. "Vendor due diligence is mostly SOC 2 reports and legal contract review..."
4. "No central inventory exists. We'd need to survey every department..."

You call the FCA: "Can we get an extension?" They respond: "30 days is standard. Banks prepared for AI governance should have this readily available."

𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝟯𝟬 𝗱𝗮𝘆𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗱𝘂𝗰𝗲 𝗱𝗼𝗰𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝗲𝘅𝗶𝘀𝘁.

𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗯𝗹𝗲𝗺: Regulators are shifting from "thinking about AI" to "show us your controls." The FCA, PRA, and EU supervisors are conducting thematic reviews, asking specific questions, and expecting comprehensive documentation.

𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗱𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲:
1. Centralized AI system inventory with classification (high-risk vs. low-risk per AI Act)
2. Evidence-based risk assessments (not just vendor promises)
3. Bias testing results with methodology documentation
4. Model governance that meets SR 11-7 or PRA SS1/23 standards
5. Third-party vendor risk assessments beyond SOC 2

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗡𝗼𝘄:
1. AI Act (2025) creates explicit documentation requirements for high-risk AI
2. FCA Dear CEO letters imminent on AI governance (based on 2024-25 speeches)
2. PRA SS1/23 extends model risk management to AI/ML systems
4. First enforcement actions coming: regulators have been "understanding," now they're "enforcing"

𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲𝘀: 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗼𝗽𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀, 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝗮𝗱:
1. Submit incomplete documentation → Regulator questions your AI governance maturity → Heightened supervision
2. Scramble to create documentation in 30 days → Rush job, low quality, doesn't withstand scrutiny
3. Request extension → Signals unpreparedness, invites more intensive review

Your reputation as CRO hinges on this. 𝗔𝗿𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗱?

𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗢𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝟮𝟳, 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟱.

𝗜𝗳 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗱𝗼 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝗘𝘂𝗿𝗼𝗽𝗲, 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗹𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗱𝘆 𝟵 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗵𝘀 𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲.
𝗜𝗳 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗿𝗲 𝗮 𝗨𝗦 𝗽𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘆 𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗜, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗘𝗖 𝗶𝘀 𝘄𝗮𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴.

If you operate in financial services, multiple deadlines just passed or are days away.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝘄𝗲 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗮𝗿𝗲:
→ DORA (EU financial services): 9 months overdue, went live January 17
→ EU AI Act: Q3 enforcement started, supervisors sending inquiries NOW
→ GDPR + AI: Active enforcement on automated decision-making
→ NYDFS (US financial): 5 days until November 1 deadline
→ Colorado AI Act: 8 months until June 2026
→ SEC AI disclosure: Active enforcement on "AI-washing"

You're not preparing for future regulations. 𝗬𝗼𝘂'𝗿𝗲 𝗔𝗟𝗥𝗘𝗔𝗗𝗬 𝗻𝗼𝗻-𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗻𝘁.

Three CROs from different sectors told me the same story:
"Board asked in September: 'Are we compliant?' We said 'we're working on it.' They said 'that's not an answer. Fix it now.'"

So they finally did discovery.
𝗧𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘆 𝗵𝗮𝗱 𝟴 𝗔𝗜 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝘀. 
𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 𝟰𝟳.

Marketing's using AI copywriters. Sales has AI SDRs. HR has resume screening. Customer service has chatbots. Finance has forecasting models. Operations has scheduling AI. Legal has contract review.

Nobody coordinated. Nobody assessed risk. Nobody documented anything.

𝗔𝗻𝗱 𝗻𝗼𝘄:
- EU supervisors requesting AI system inventories (30-day response)
- US regulators asking about AI governance frameworks
- Insurance underwriters demanding risk data (or 40% premium increases)
- Boards demanding answers in Q4 meetings (happening NOW)
- 2026 budgets being finalized - AI projects blocked without governance

The companies that moved in Q2-Q3? Ready. Compliant. Confident.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗱? 𝗡𝗢𝗪 𝗽𝗮𝘆𝗶𝗻𝗴:
✗ Emergency consulting rates (3x normal fees)
✗ Daily board pressure
✗ Regulatory inquiries
✗ Frozen AI initiatives

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵:
You can't achieve full compliance overnight.

𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗖𝗔𝗡:
✓ Start this week and demonstrate good faith to regulators
✓ Get operational before year-end
✓ Be ready for 2026 deadlines
✓ Tell your Board: "We've engaged, here's the plan"

Or wait until December, miss Q4 budget, and start 2026 already non-compliant.

Every executive says the same thing: "I thought we had time."

You don't.

The regulations aren't coming. They're here. You're late.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗯𝗼𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆𝗲𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗸?

Software isn't a moat. 

Skills are.

We launched our skills engine today with 100s out of the box, built for finance.

If you want your AI workflows and agents to be perform better, you probably need to add a bit more *Flow Control*.

Flow control is what tells a workflow when to do something, whether to do it, and how many times.

Adding more explicit flow control will make your workflow more predictable, reliable, and probably cheaper too.

The 3 most common flow control concepts are:

1) Path - Take different actions based on a condition

2) Wait - Wait for some action to occur before proceeding

3) Loop - Perform the same action(s) for each item in a list

I went to Ecuador to switch off.

The first few days, AI followed me anyway 🤖.

I kept thinking about a new skill I wanted to build. Even made notes on my phone of what to include on the plane to Quito 🙈.

But I refused to build it on holiday, and luckily, I couldn't really without my laptop.

In the last few months, the feeling of "five new things to learn with AI" and "what are all these new terms and tools?!" has been a lot.

Ironically, not working on any of it for two weeks actually made me feel better.
Reminding me there is more to life than AI (like swimming with penguins 🐧).

Two days back, that feeling crept in again. I even panic-bought an AI Accelerator subscription 😅.

Then I read an article by Ethan Garr about how, even though he's in the top 1% of AI builders, he still feels behind.

I think a lot of us have been quietly feeling this but not saying it out loud. The pressure to keep up with AI now feels like a second job.

I don't want to go back into that spiral and feel like I need time off again immediately (especially since my next holiday is quite a while away).

So I'm going to try his fix in the coming months:

1️⃣ An evaluation layer - what do and don't I need to learn
2️⃣ Build a backlog
3️⃣ Dedicated discovery /experimentation time per week, vs trying to squeeze it in every day

Kind of like a growth process.

Coming back, that's the energy I want to bring. Step by step. Curious, not frantic.

The AI changes are big, and they're not slowing down. But we also don't have to do it all at once.

Anyone else feeling this way?

Meta internal AI agent exposed sensitive data this month. No external attacker. No jailbreak. The agent simply exceeded its intended scope.

This is the most dangerous category of AI incident, agents that are already inside, with valid credentials, doing things nobody authorized. Not because they're malicious. Because nobody defined the boundaries at the infrastructure level.

Prompt-level guardrails don't solve this. A prompt is a suggestion, not a constraint. When your agent acts at 3am with nobody watching, the prompt isn't checking permissions. Nobody is.

65% of organizations have already had an AI agent security incident (CSA). Most weren't attacks. They were agents acting autonomously without anyone defining what they're allowed to do at a level the agent can't override.

This is what we're solving at Vorim AI:
→ Cryptographic identity per agent — you always know which agent took which action
→ Permissions enforced per action in sub-5ms — the agent can't reason its way around a denied check
→ Every action signed with the agent's key — tamper-proof evidence, not log files
→ Trust scores that degrade in real-time when behavior drifts
→ Instant revocation — one call, zero blast radius

The question isn't whether this will happen to your company. It's whether you'll have the identity layer in place when it does.

One metric we track obsessively is found time. It's the number of days between a role going live on Paraform and when the hiring manager meets the candidate they eventually hire.

Not time-to-hire, which includes everything downstream like scheduling, multiple interview rounds, and offer negotiations. Those timelines vary a lot depending on a company's process. An SDR role might close in weeks, while a senior marketing hire might take months with multiple steps and onsites.

Found time measures what we control: how fast can we get the right person in front of customers.

Our average found time across all of our roles is less than 12 days.

That number started with the marketplace itself. Thousands of specialized recruiters, each with warm candidate networks, all competing to move fast on every role. When you post a role on Paraform, you're not waiting for one agency to start sourcing from scratch. You have access to specialized recruiters who already have warm connections with candidates that are actively looking.

A big change that's been pushing found time down is ParaAI (our intelligence layer). Based on your preferences, role details, and candidate information it scans our entire recruiter network and their vetted candidates to surfaces great fits who're warm and ready to interview. Our algorithms then learn from your hiring preferences and adapts in real-time to deliver you better candidates.

Hiring managers using ParaAI are filling their pipelines with great candidates on day 1. One company had 4 first round interviews within 3 days of opening a role on Paraform - and a hire within 2 weeks.

Traditional agencies are materially limited by the size of their candidate network. They often source one pool, for one client, one role at a time and often take weeks to outbound + source candidates. 

We're surfacing warm, interview-ready candidates within days and redefining what fast really means in hiring.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗕𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗦𝗽𝗼𝘁: 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗖𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗗𝗲𝗳𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗪𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝗦𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗬𝗼𝘂

Last Tuesday at 3:47 AM, a financial services CTO's fraud detection system failed. Not from a cyberattack. Not from a server outage. 

A third-party API three layers deep changed its output format. Six downstream systems failed. Cost: £340,000 before anyone noticed.

The CTO had invested millions in Fortinet and SOC 2 compliance. None of it caught this failure.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘂𝗻𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝘁𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵: 𝗔𝗜 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗶𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝗰𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸.
Cyber Risk = Perimeter Defence
AI Risk = Immune System Defence

𝗙𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗴𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗜𝗡. 𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗮𝗻'𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻:
→ Your customer service AI hallucinates product features
→ Your credit model drifts 40% in accuracy over 6 months
→ Your pricing algorithm learns to discriminate against protected classes

𝗔𝗜 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗡𝗘𝗪 𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝗰𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗘𝗨 𝗔𝗜 𝗔𝗰𝘁 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗲𝗽𝘁 "𝘄𝗲 𝗱𝗼 𝗔𝗜 𝗴𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲." 𝗜𝘁 𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗱𝘀:
• Risk classification of every system
• Bias testing with documented methodology
• Training data provenance
• Continuous monitoring (not annual audits)
• Penalties: Up to 7% of global turnover

DORA requires you to map dependencies 3+ layers deep and answer: "If this AI vendor fails, what else breaks?"

𝗠𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗖𝗧𝗢𝘀 𝗰𝗮𝗻'𝘁 𝗮𝗻𝘀𝘄𝗲𝗿:
❌ "How many high-risk AI systems do we operate?"
❌ "Show me bias testing for customer-facing AI"
❌ "What's our financial exposure if OpenAI goes down?"

**68% of ML models degrade within 6 months.** Annual audits are obsolete the day they're completed.

𝗔𝗜 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗿𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗶𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗻𝘂𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗶𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗲 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴:
✅ Complete inventory (including shadow AI)
✅ 81 risk factors across 6 dimensions
✅ 24/7 drift and performance monitoring
✅ Dependency mapping for cascade failures
✅ Financial quantification in $, not "High/Medium/Low"
✅ Regulatory precision: specific article citations

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗮𝗽𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲:
• The board asks questions you can't answer
• The regulator sends a letter you can't respond to
• The incident that costs £340K before breakfast

𝗙𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝗲𝘅𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸𝘀. 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗔𝗜 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗯𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻. governr.

BIG ONE 🏆 
 
Everyone told us we’d fail: “pick a lane.. Sales OR CS - you can’t do both.”

Today, I’m THRILLED to share - for the first time ever, Aligned is the only platform G2 ranked as #1 in BOTH Digital Sales Room AND Client Portal.

1,166 verified reviews from the people who actually use the product daily.

Here’s why the bet worked - and why I’d make it again:

——

There is no Sales team. There is no CS team.

There's a Revenue team.

We treat them like they’re different. They’re NOT.

We built tools to support each of them. They only end up helping their managers.

The Sales platform (CRM, Revenue Intelligence…) was built for sales managers, not sellers. Reporting tools in a sales costume.

The CS platform was built for renewal forecasts, not customers. A retention spreadsheet in a CS costume.

Both didn’t fix the actual deal workflow. Same win rates. Same renewal rates.

Both definitely did not do much for buyers or customers.

But the top performers in both roles look the same:

Top AEs don’t "sell." Like CSMs, they project-manage deals, build relationships, make buying easy and play for long-term success - not just short-term revenue.

Top CSMs aren’t glorified "support hotline." Like AEs, they multithread, run discovery, build champions and business cases, and defend renewals like quota.

It's the same job.

The org chart - and tools - just haven't caught up.

We built Aligned for the true heroes of the revenue org - the reps.

Whether it’s AEs, CSMs, SEs, or Partner Managers.

One AI workspace for running deals from meeting one, to the renewal.

A better deal workflow that solves execution gaps, fixes the buying experience, and delivers proven win rate and retention results.

We didn't pick a lane because the lane is the problem.

The siloed manager-pushed tools era is ending.

We're building the system of action that comes next 🦾

——

To our customers - 1,166 of you wrote a review. Humbled doesn't cover it. To our team - you gave your heart and soul to building a truly great product and business. You built the future.

Onward.

When I posted about paid on LinkedIn a few weeks ago, I got several comments along the lines of "Don't start on paid until you're killing it on organic." Disagree!

This is bad advice. You should apply learnings from organic to paid and from paid to organic.

The best way to do this is to take an audience-first approach to all channels you are running, including paid.

AI makes it so much easier to have an enriched list of accounts and contacts, dynamically feed this into platforms (including LinkedIn and search), and take signals from those channels to enrich this list even more.

Build this out and all of your learnings compound. Better audience data yields better ad performance, which produces better signals, which makes audiences even better.

Here are some ways you can make sure paid learnings are driving organic learnings and vice versa:

1. SEM test → SEO focus areas. SEM is often the fastest way to test if a keyword is worth investing in on SEO and AEO.
2. De-anonymized visitors → outbound + retargeting → return visits. Play around with sequencing ads and outbound once someone visits your site (and revisits your site).
3. Closed-won → lookalikes. Build lookalikes off actual closed-won accounts, feed those into ad platforms.

More thoughts on breaking down paid silos and taking an audience-first approach in my latest newsletter: https://lnkd.in/gUDNjcgd

content_text:
𝗦𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴'𝘀 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝗠&𝗔 𝗱𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝟭𝟴 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗵𝘀.

Every strategic buyer and PE acquirer now asks the same question: "Show me your AI risk documentation."

And honestly? Most portfolio companies have nothing to show. 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗴𝗮𝗽 𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘆 𝗮𝘁 𝗲𝘅𝗶𝘁.

𝗟𝗲𝘁 𝗺𝗲 𝗴𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲.
1. Financial services company, £800M offer on the table. Everything looks great, strong unit economics, solid growth trajectory, experienced team. 

2. Six weeks into DD, buyer's counsel asks for AI governance documentation. Specifically: vendor risk assessments, bias testing results, monitoring protocols for the third-party AI systems.

3. The company has... nothing. No formal assessments. No testing documentation. Just vendor contracts.

4. Buyer's response wasn't to walk away. It was to restructure the deal: £250M in escrow for contingent AI liability, plus an 18-month earn out tied to governance remediation.

If you're holding 15%, you just watched £37M of your realized value move into escrow and earnout. Money you thought you'd have at close is now locked up—potentially for years.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘄.
The EU AI Act made something very clear: if your company deploys AI systems, you own the liability. 

So when a buyer acquires your portfolio company, they inherit the exposure to every AI system in production. If those systems have compliance gaps or undiscovered bias, the buyer is on the hook for regulatory fines, customer litigation.

Smart buyers know this. They're pricing it into their offers. The typical adjustment? 20-40% of enterprise value with high AI exposure and weak governance documentation. 

𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝗮 𝗳𝗹𝗶𝗽 𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀.
Companies that have their AI governance buttoned up are getting rewarded. Buyers increasingly view mature AI risk management as a sign of operational excellence. 

The result is clean exits at full valuation. No escrow haircuts. No extended earnouts. No painful reps and warranties insurance negotiations.

For IPOs, this becomes even more critical. Underwriters won't take you public with material undisclosed AI liability sitting in your S-1. 

𝗦𝗼 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗲?
If you have portfolio companies approaching exit windows in the next 18-24 months, AI governance documentation should be on your value creation plan—alongside the playbook of revenue growth and margin expansion.

Cost is around £100K-£150K per company. Value protection on a £750M exit with 20% buyer discount? That's £150M you would have left on the table.

It's not really about compliance, it's about capturing full value when you sell.
Because buyers are asking the question. And if your portfolio companies don't have it, you're going to feel it in the price.

Seating chart builder with Clay? When we host dinners we rank everyone based their SFDC activity and enriched data we have on the contact.

Depending on if they are an Executive Buyer, Champion, Primary User, Existing Customer or Partner we strategically place them around our tables.

Our events team uses a vibe coded app that feeds off this data to rearrange guests. This allows our ClayDR team to follow up both internally and externally with the right context.

After the event concludes an automated system pings the Clay hosts that were sitting closest to the guest to understand what the conversation was. The host enters that information and it's synced back into SFDC.

Externally that context gets inserted into copy that is preloaded for the team to review before it's sent to the guest the next day.

That allows us to "carry on the conversation" and has resulted in measurably higher meeting bookings post-event than our old system of hosting events and generically following up with guests after.

Field marketing is a perfect use case for a system that combines layers of automation and tooling with human feedback and intervention. As we have focused on "in person" as a key channel this year we have iterated on a number of flows that make this motion sing. Right now in person events are our highest converting channel.

Let me know other tips y'all have for automating around the real world! Curious what other events teams or GTM Engineers working on events are doing.

As AI adoption advances, institutions are moving beyond ad-hoc reviews toward scalable and controlled AI risk management.

➡️ End-to-end recipe to operationalize FINMA guidelines within banks 🏦
➡️ Clear controls for performance, hallucinations, security & more
➡️ Repeatable blueprint for consistent, auditable AI evaluations

⚙️ 𝐓𝐰𝐨-𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐭 𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐚𝐜𝐡:
1️⃣ 𝐀𝐬𝐬𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 🧩 – Evaluates AI architecture, deployment, capabilities, and performance against FINMA guidance; identifies gaps and creates a consistent, auditable blueprint.
2️⃣ 𝐈𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 🧠 – Translates high-level governance into technical controls and continuous validation checks, enabling informed, risk-based decisions throughout the AI lifecycle.

A great example of Switzerland advancing trustworthy and compliant AI governance.

Major question for VCs today: does your fund size still make sense?

𝗧𝗵𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿-𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀

• Do I lead rounds? 
  
  If I do, the median lead check is 25%-50% larger today than it was only 3 years back. Am I willing to invest into fewer companies (building a more concentrated portfolio) in order to maintain ownership?
  
• Do I follow-on? 
  
  If I lead, I'm sort of expected to (side note as Peter - kinda think this is an expectation that should fade but I get it). Well, great, the follow-on check is ALSO much larger than it used to be and if my portco gets marked up by a major Tier 1 fund, forget it there goes millions. 
  
• Do I bridge companies? 
  
  A bridge here, another there, and pretty soon you're talking about real money that could have been spent on another net new bet. But I need to maintain good relationships with my founders in this business. 

Tricky all the way down. But the incentives clearly point in one direction - try and raise a bigger fund next time. 

Which LPs always love 😅

After nearly 20 years in analytics, one thing has always been true: the people closest to the data and the people making the decisions are rarely the same people. I've seen it play out at every company I've worked with. Data in one room, strategy in another.

What's changing now is that AI is starting to blur those lines in a real and practical way. Product, growth, and marketing are getting harder to separate, and the analysts and practitioners I see doing well aren't waiting for their role to be redefined for them. They're figuring it out as they go, building new skills, taking on new ownership, and staying curious about where the work is actually heading.

On June 30, Adam Fishman and I are talking through what this actually looks like in practice as part of Amplitude's AI Analytics for Builders series. We'll get into the specific skills that matter, where ownership is shifting, and what AI fluency really means for analytics and product roles right now.

How to Stay Relevant as a Product, Growth, and Marketing Blur 
Register on Maven | Wed, June 30 → https://lnkd.in/gyfbrHwA

#AIAnalyticsforBuilders

𝗦𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴'𝘀 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝗠&𝗔 𝗱𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝟭𝟴 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗵𝘀.

Every strategic buyer and PE acquirer now asks the same question: "Show me your AI risk documentation."

And honestly? Most portfolio companies have nothing to show. 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗴𝗮𝗽 𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘆 𝗮𝘁 𝗲𝘅𝗶𝘁.

𝗟𝗲𝘁 𝗺𝗲 𝗴𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲.
1. Financial services company, £800M offer on the table. Everything looks great, strong unit economics, solid growth trajectory, experienced team. 

2. Six weeks into DD, buyer's counsel asks for AI governance documentation. Specifically: vendor risk assessments, bias testing results, monitoring protocols for the third-party AI systems.

3. The company has... nothing. No formal assessments. No testing documentation. Just vendor contracts.

4. Buyer's response wasn't to walk away. It was to restructure the deal: £250M in escrow for contingent AI liability, plus an 18-month earn out tied to governance remediation.

If you're holding 15%, you just watched £37M of your realized value move into escrow and earnout. Money you thought you'd have at close is now locked up—potentially for years.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘄.
The EU AI Act made something very clear: if your company deploys AI systems, you own the liability. 

So when a buyer acquires your portfolio company, they inherit the exposure to every AI system in production. If those systems have compliance gaps or undiscovered bias, the buyer is on the hook for regulatory fines, customer litigation.

Smart buyers know this. They're pricing it into their offers. The typical adjustment? 20-40% of enterprise value with high AI exposure and weak governance documentation. 

𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝗮 𝗳𝗹𝗶𝗽 𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀.
Companies that have their AI governance buttoned up are getting rewarded. Buyers increasingly view mature AI risk management as a sign of operational excellence. 

The result is clean exits at full valuation. No escrow haircuts. No extended earnouts. No painful reps and warranties insurance negotiations.

For IPOs, this becomes even more critical. Underwriters won't take you public with material undisclosed AI liability sitting in your S-1. 

𝗦𝗼 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗲?
If you have portfolio companies approaching exit windows in the next 18-24 months, AI governance documentation should be on your value creation plan—alongside the playbook of revenue growth and margin expansion.

Cost is around £100K-£150K per company. Value protection on a £750M exit with 20% buyer discount? That's £150M you would have left on the table.

It's not really about compliance, it's about capturing full value when you sell.
Because buyers are asking the question. And if your portfolio companies don't have it, you're going to feel it in the price.

An accountant in Vancouver just saved us $120K a year. Prescott Thompson got tired of a close-management vendor. So he built his own using our internal AI platform Mesh.

Mesh is our internal AI productivity platform / AI coworker that gives every employee a highly configured agent connected to ZoomInfo’s internal context, systems, data, and workflows.

From accountants to sales reps to engineers - Mesh makes every employee a builder who can create AI tools tailored to their own workflows, rather than being a “button pusher” using tools made by someone else.

Prescott built PressClose - a full financial close management app with task management across 65 accountants, variance analysis, reconciliation tracking, audit trails, and direct SAP integration. The vendor we were paying $120K/year for was capped at 65 users, had no SAP integration, and our feature requests kept getting met with "it's on the roadmap."

Prescott's version does more AND it cost us $0 in incremental licensing.

Prescott isn't an outlier. He's one of HUNDREDS of people at ZoomInfo building micro-apps on top of ZI Mesh. A few that particularly impressed me:

- Zachary Day, an AE in Chicago, is building Company Map - when he's traveling it shows every ICP-matching company within 5 miles, layered with intent signals. He's booking meetings between flights.

- Jessica Buchwald (Evans)'s marketing ops team built a campaign generator that took personalized sequence creation from 1.5 hours to 2 minutes per campaign.

- Sam D'Arcy in product built a voice-of-customer engine that reads every sales call and pulls out feature requests so PMs don't have to manually review hundreds of calls.

- Sarah Austin on our cash application team built an AR Payment Matcher that recovered a $16K payment that had been sitting unidentified for over a year.

None of these were on our roadmap. Nobody pinged me on slack asking for my permission. They just saw something broken in their day and fixed it.

We now have 550+ micro-apps across the company, built on ZI Mesh.

All built BOTTOMS UP.

If you enable the person who understands the problem to also build the solution, EVERYTHING gets faster. You don’t waste time on spec docs, hand offs or have to translate the use case through 3 layers of PMs and engineers etc etc

For leaders implementing AI, our job is to make sure the data infrastructure and security exists AND the people who build something valuable get recognized for it.

Do those things and your team will surprise you.

Leave your data where the business needs it.

Wordsmith maps everything out and automatically pulls context from the right places preserving folder structure, version history, and your original permissions.

Wordsmith comes to you.

content_text:
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗕𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗦𝗽𝗼𝘁: 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗖𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗗𝗲𝗳𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗪𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝗦𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗬𝗼𝘂

Last Tuesday at 3:47 AM, a financial services CTO's fraud detection system failed. Not from a cyberattack. Not from a server outage. 

A third-party API three layers deep changed its output format. Six downstream systems failed. Cost: £340,000 before anyone noticed.

The CTO had invested millions in Fortinet and SOC 2 compliance. None of it caught this failure.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘂𝗻𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝘁𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵: 𝗔𝗜 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗶𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝗰𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸.
Cyber Risk = Perimeter Defence
AI Risk = Immune System Defence

𝗙𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗴𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗜𝗡. 𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗮𝗻'𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻:
→ Your customer service AI hallucinates product features
→ Your credit model drifts 40% in accuracy over 6 months
→ Your pricing algorithm learns to discriminate against protected classes

𝗔𝗜 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗡𝗘𝗪 𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝗰𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗘𝗨 𝗔𝗜 𝗔𝗰𝘁 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗲𝗽𝘁 "𝘄𝗲 𝗱𝗼 𝗔𝗜 𝗴𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲." 𝗜𝘁 𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗱𝘀:
• Risk classification of every system
• Bias testing with documented methodology
• Training data provenance
• Continuous monitoring (not annual audits)
• Penalties: Up to 7% of global turnover

DORA requires you to map dependencies 3+ layers deep and answer: "If this AI vendor fails, what else breaks?"

𝗠𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗖𝗧𝗢𝘀 𝗰𝗮𝗻'𝘁 𝗮𝗻𝘀𝘄𝗲𝗿:
❌ "How many high-risk AI systems do we operate?"
❌ "Show me bias testing for customer-facing AI"
❌ "What's our financial exposure if OpenAI goes down?"

**68% of ML models degrade within 6 months.** Annual audits are obsolete the day they're completed.

𝗔𝗜 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗿𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗶𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗻𝘂𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗶𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗲 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴:
✅ Complete inventory (including shadow AI)
✅ 81 risk factors across 6 dimensions
✅ 24/7 drift and performance monitoring
✅ Dependency mapping for cascade failures
✅ Financial quantification in $, not "High/Medium/Low"
✅ Regulatory precision: specific article citations

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗮𝗽𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲:
• The board asks questions you can't answer
• The regulator sends a letter you can't respond to
• The incident that costs £340K before breakfast

𝗙𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝗲𝘅𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸𝘀. 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗔𝗜 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗯𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻. governr.

Government compliance: not easy, but easier.
