creator_name: Analytics and Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Marketing and Retail
content_text:
𝗦𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴'𝘀 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝗠&𝗔 𝗱𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘁 𝟭𝟴 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗵𝘀.

Every strategic buyer and PE acquirer now asks the same question: "Show me your AI risk documentation."

And honestly? Most portfolio companies have nothing to show. 𝗧𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗴𝗮𝗽 𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘆 𝗮𝘁 𝗲𝘅𝗶𝘁.

𝗟𝗲𝘁 𝗺𝗲 𝗴𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲.
1. Financial services company, £800M offer on the table. Everything looks great, strong unit economics, solid growth trajectory, experienced team. 

2. Six weeks into DD, buyer's counsel asks for AI governance documentation. Specifically: vendor risk assessments, bias testing results, monitoring protocols for the third-party AI systems.

3. The company has... nothing. No formal assessments. No testing documentation. Just vendor contracts.

4. Buyer's response wasn't to walk away. It was to restructure the deal: £250M in escrow for contingent AI liability, plus an 18-month earn out tied to governance remediation.

If you're holding 15%, you just watched £37M of your realized value move into escrow and earnout. Money you thought you'd have at close is now locked up—potentially for years.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘄.
The EU AI Act made something very clear: if your company deploys AI systems, you own the liability. 

So when a buyer acquires your portfolio company, they inherit the exposure to every AI system in production. If those systems have compliance gaps or undiscovered bias, the buyer is on the hook for regulatory fines, customer litigation.

Smart buyers know this. They're pricing it into their offers. The typical adjustment? 20-40% of enterprise value with high AI exposure and weak governance documentation. 

𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝗮 𝗳𝗹𝗶𝗽 𝘀𝗶𝗱𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀.
Companies that have their AI governance buttoned up are getting rewarded. Buyers increasingly view mature AI risk management as a sign of operational excellence. 

The result is clean exits at full valuation. No escrow haircuts. No extended earnouts. No painful reps and warranties insurance negotiations.

For IPOs, this becomes even more critical. Underwriters won't take you public with material undisclosed AI liability sitting in your S-1. 

𝗦𝗼 𝘄𝗵𝗮𝘁'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗲?
If you have portfolio companies approaching exit windows in the next 18-24 months, AI governance documentation should be on your value creation plan—alongside the playbook of revenue growth and margin expansion.

Cost is around £100K-£150K per company. Value protection on a £750M exit with 20% buyer discount? That's £150M you would have left on the table.

It's not really about compliance, it's about capturing full value when you sell.
Because buyers are asking the question. And if your portfolio companies don't have it, you're going to feel it in the price.

BRiMM is opening its doors - and we want you in first

After months of building in the open, sharing our ups and downs, and getting brilliant feedback from this community, BRiMM's founder membership wait list has launched. 

But here's the thing - we're not launching like everyone else.

Most startups raise money and pour it into Facebook ads, lining Mark Zuckerberg's pockets. We're doing something different.

Our first external shareholder? Not an external investor but Mother Nature herself. She will own 10% of BRiMM and have a seat on our board (represented by Lawyers for Nature).

And instead of paying for adverts, we're asking you to help us grow through genuine connection. And in return we’re offering you a founder membership and an opportunity with it to shape a revolutionary new model. 

We’re committing everything we make from the founder membership to our Planet Fund - 100% of it - to help the planet-positive brands we stock lower their footprints faster.

Here's what founder membership gets you:

- £80 annual credit for life
- 10% cash back on everything
- Member-only content and events
- A vote on which climate initiatives we support

But more importantly, you're part of something bigger. 

A collective of climate optimists proving that you don't have to choose between joy and impact.

We're not trying to be ‘the everything store’. We're the enough store. Curated, vetted products that are good for you, good for your home, and good for the planet.

Starting with Reset Boxes for beauty & personal care, pantry, and home cleaning - because a planet-positive life begins with resetting your everyday.

Don’t get me wrong - we’ll need to raise some money, and yes, we’ll have to advertise at some point (even planet-positive missions need airtime). But for this first step, we wanted to challenge the norm and launch in a way that matched our mission.

The reality? Sustainability doesn't sell right now. That's exactly why we need to reframe it. This isn't about sacrifice - it's about abundance. Living bright whilst treading light.

Ready to join our founder members and help us prove that business can be a force for good?

Join the waitlist (link in comments) and bring some friends. Every membership funds real change.

James 🌍 💚

–

Follow me (James Haycock) to hear the highs (hopefully) and lows (inevitably) of building a new, purposeful business. You can also sign up for our weekly dispatches - planet positive living straight to your inbox (link in comments).

𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝗢𝗰𝘁𝗼𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝟮𝟳, 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟱.

𝗜𝗳 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗱𝗼 𝗯𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝗘𝘂𝗿𝗼𝗽𝗲, 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗹𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗱𝘆 𝟵 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗵𝘀 𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗲.
𝗜𝗳 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗿𝗲 𝗮 𝗨𝗦 𝗽𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘆 𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗔𝗜, 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗘𝗖 𝗶𝘀 𝘄𝗮𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴.

If you operate in financial services, multiple deadlines just passed or are days away.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝘄𝗲 𝗮𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗮𝗿𝗲:
→ DORA (EU financial services): 9 months overdue, went live January 17
→ EU AI Act: Q3 enforcement started, supervisors sending inquiries NOW
→ GDPR + AI: Active enforcement on automated decision-making
→ NYDFS (US financial): 5 days until November 1 deadline
→ Colorado AI Act: 8 months until June 2026
→ SEC AI disclosure: Active enforcement on "AI-washing"

You're not preparing for future regulations. 𝗬𝗼𝘂'𝗿𝗲 𝗔𝗟𝗥𝗘𝗔𝗗𝗬 𝗻𝗼𝗻-𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗻𝘁.

Three CROs from different sectors told me the same story:
"Board asked in September: 'Are we compliant?' We said 'we're working on it.' They said 'that's not an answer. Fix it now.'"

So they finally did discovery.
𝗧𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝘆 𝗵𝗮𝗱 𝟴 𝗔𝗜 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺𝘀. 
𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 𝟰𝟳.

Marketing's using AI copywriters. Sales has AI SDRs. HR has resume screening. Customer service has chatbots. Finance has forecasting models. Operations has scheduling AI. Legal has contract review.

Nobody coordinated. Nobody assessed risk. Nobody documented anything.

𝗔𝗻𝗱 𝗻𝗼𝘄:
- EU supervisors requesting AI system inventories (30-day response)
- US regulators asking about AI governance frameworks
- Insurance underwriters demanding risk data (or 40% premium increases)
- Boards demanding answers in Q4 meetings (happening NOW)
- 2026 budgets being finalized - AI projects blocked without governance

The companies that moved in Q2-Q3? Ready. Compliant. Confident.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘄𝗮𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗱? 𝗡𝗢𝗪 𝗽𝗮𝘆𝗶𝗻𝗴:
✗ Emergency consulting rates (3x normal fees)
✗ Daily board pressure
✗ Regulatory inquiries
✗ Frozen AI initiatives

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘁𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵:
You can't achieve full compliance overnight.

𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗖𝗔𝗡:
✓ Start this week and demonstrate good faith to regulators
✓ Get operational before year-end
✓ Be ready for 2026 deadlines
✓ Tell your Board: "We've engaged, here's the plan"

Or wait until December, miss Q4 budget, and start 2026 already non-compliant.

Every executive says the same thing: "I thought we had time."

You don't.

The regulations aren't coming. They're here. You're late.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝘁𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗯𝗼𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗲𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗼𝘆𝗲𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗸?

content_text:
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗜 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗕𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗦𝗽𝗼𝘁: 𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗖𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗗𝗲𝗳𝗲𝗻𝘀𝗲𝘀 𝗪𝗼𝗻'𝘁 𝗦𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗬𝗼𝘂

Last Tuesday at 3:47 AM, a financial services CTO's fraud detection system failed. Not from a cyberattack. Not from a server outage. 

A third-party API three layers deep changed its output format. Six downstream systems failed. Cost: £340,000 before anyone noticed.

The CTO had invested millions in Fortinet and SOC 2 compliance. None of it caught this failure.

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲'𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘂𝗻𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝘁𝗿𝘂𝘁𝗵: 𝗔𝗜 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗶𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝗰𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸.
Cyber Risk = Perimeter Defence
AI Risk = Immune System Defence

𝗙𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗴𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗜𝗡. 𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝗶𝘁 𝗰𝗮𝗻'𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗲 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝗻:
→ Your customer service AI hallucinates product features
→ Your credit model drifts 40% in accuracy over 6 months
→ Your pricing algorithm learns to discriminate against protected classes

𝗔𝗜 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗡𝗘𝗪 𝗹𝗮𝘆𝗲𝗿 𝗯𝗲𝘁𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗻 𝗰𝘆𝗯𝗲𝗿 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗘𝗨 𝗔𝗜 𝗔𝗰𝘁 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝗮𝗰𝗰𝗲𝗽𝘁 "𝘄𝗲 𝗱𝗼 𝗔𝗜 𝗴𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲." 𝗜𝘁 𝗱𝗲𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗱𝘀:
• Risk classification of every system
• Bias testing with documented methodology
• Training data provenance
• Continuous monitoring (not annual audits)
• Penalties: Up to 7% of global turnover

DORA requires you to map dependencies 3+ layers deep and answer: "If this AI vendor fails, what else breaks?"

𝗠𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗖𝗧𝗢𝘀 𝗰𝗮𝗻'𝘁 𝗮𝗻𝘀𝘄𝗲𝗿:
❌ "How many high-risk AI systems do we operate?"
❌ "Show me bias testing for customer-facing AI"
❌ "What's our financial exposure if OpenAI goes down?"

**68% of ML models degrade within 6 months.** Annual audits are obsolete the day they're completed.

𝗔𝗜 𝗿𝗶𝘀𝗸 𝗿𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗶𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗻𝘂𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗶𝗺𝗺𝘂𝗻𝗲 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴:
✅ Complete inventory (including shadow AI)
✅ 81 risk factors across 6 dimensions
✅ 24/7 drift and performance monitoring
✅ Dependency mapping for cascade failures
✅ Financial quantification in $, not "High/Medium/Low"
✅ Regulatory precision: specific article citations

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗾𝘂𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀𝗻'𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗰𝗮𝗽𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆. 𝗜𝘁'𝘀 𝘄𝗵𝗲𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗿 𝘆𝗼𝘂'𝗹𝗹 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗶𝘁 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲:
• The board asks questions you can't answer
• The regulator sends a letter you can't respond to
• The incident that costs £340K before breakfast

𝗙𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝗲𝘅𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝘁𝘁𝗮𝗰𝗸𝘀. 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝗻𝗳𝗿𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗽𝘀 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗔𝗜 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗯𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻. governr.

💡Interesting take aways from the excellent 2026 European Deep Tech Report by Walden Catalyst Ventures, Lakestar and Dealroom.co:

➡️ Almost all founders of deep tech startups have a university education, with 1/3 having completed a PhD, 73% a master's degree, etc. Very different from non-deep tech companies and different than what some online gurus suggest sometimes. :)

➡️ Strong education *and* research institutions are key to a thriving deep tech ecosystem, as the people who create the companies come from these institutions.

➡️ ETH Zürich and EPFL are doing great: they have an amazing world-class education and research environment, which allows them to attract top talent worldwide and to develop both local and foreign talent to the world-class level. These are the people who then end up creating the deep-tech startups. 

➡️ The No. 1 priority of any country that wants a true deep-tech ecosystem is creating world-class research and education institutions. Too often, I see people talking about deep tech, even at a national level, but they lack the world-class research, hence, deep tech is not in their future, despite how many times they repeat the word "innovation", "tech transfer", "AI" or whatever. :) 

➡️ INSAIT - Institute for Computer Science, Artificial Intelligence and Technology in Sofia, created in partnership with ETH Zürich and EPFL, is a great example of following this same successful model, but in Bulgaria. Its a disruptive model, but its how things should be done organically and properly if one wants to create a foundation for a true deep-tech ecosystem.

OpenAI and Anthropic just admitted they cannot win the application layer 😳

a16z spotted the signal: nobody pours billions into forward-deployed joint ventures if they think the next model release will solve everything

The mobile internet played out the exact same way:

▫️ First the semis won (Qualcomm, ARM)
▫️ Then infra and devices (Samsung, Apple)
▫️ Then software and services ran the table (Google, Amazon)

Value always migrates up the stack

In AI, infra is already telling us the app layer is a separate, massive opportunity the foundation labs cannot fully capture.

The next $1T companies will be built on top of the models, not by the ones building them

Where do you think the biggest AI value lives in 3 years?

Our plan is to bring automated contract workflows into the managed legal services that some of the world's largest legal teams already rely on.

For in-house legal teams working with Morae, routine and high-volume contract work gets done faster and at scale, without proportional cost growth.

Morae has spent a decade earning the trust of the GCs we want to work with. Wordsmith now shows up inside that relationship, not as a tool legal teams have to evaluate and implement themselves, but as part of a service that is already working.

Skills vs. Workflows

Skill: A set of instructions that tell an AI agent how to behave. Each time the skill is invoked, the LLM re-reads and reinterprets those instructions.

Workflow: A predefined flowchart of steps that runs automatically in response to a trigger. The workflow executes within the same structure every time and LLMs only perform the specific steps that require intelligence.

Both serve the same purpose. They capture the logic of repeated tasks so AI can execute on them consistently.

But there are important differences! Skills are typically more flexible, but workflows are typically more reliable and cheaper.

If a task needs to happen frequently and consistently, a workflow is usually a better choice.

If a task is infrequent and requires on-the-fly improvisation each time, a skill is usually a better choice.

What has Perplexity been up to last two months? We've silently been working on the next big thing: Perplexity Computer. Computer unifies every current capability of AI into a single system. Files, tools, memory, and models, orchestrated together, working for you.

It's multi-model by design. When models specialize, they just become tools similar to the file system, CLI tools, connectors, browser, search. No single model family can do its best work for you without the talents of other models.

Steve Jobs said “Musicians play their instruments, I play the orchestra.” Perplexity Computer orchestrates 19 models. One reasons, another codes, another writes, etc. Users can also set specific models for certain sub-tasks for sophisticated token management.

The computer is one of the best inventions known to mankind. And when AIs can orchestrate a file system with CLI tools + a browser (real-time internet access) + your personal connectors, AI essentially becomes the Computer, running things on the cloud as you sleep.

We're opening it initially to all Max users, and introducing usage-based pricing, which we believe is the right business model for AI instead of ads. Once we are satisfied with load tests, Pro users will be able to run jobs on Computer too.

In-house legal teams are emerging as significant pressure testers for AI-first legal work. This was a key takeaway from this week’s conversation on The Geek in Review with Alex Su and Andres Chagui of Latitude Legal, along with Greg Lambert.

While law firms have been vocal about AI adoption, clients are discussion what work should remain with outside counsel? What tasks are best kept in-house? What can be managed through AI, flexible talent, or a blend of both?

Alex highlighted that AI may gain traction more quickly within legal departments, where tasks tend to be more repeatable and the demand for efficiency is ever-present. This shift is crucial for law firms, as in-house teams are evolving into potential competitors for work traditionally assumed to belong to firms.

The importance of senior legal judgment remains intact. However, the dynamics of pricing and staffing for the work that supports this judgment are very much in flux. 

Additionally, insights from Nikki Shaver of Legaltech Hub emphasize the necessity of external perspectives as firms and legal departments navigate AI adoption, policy, knowledge management, innovation, and technology selection. #LegalTech #AIinLaw #LegalInnovation #InHouseLegal #flexiblestaffing #LawFirms #FutureOfLaw #KnowledgeManagement #TheGeekInReview #alternatecareer

Engineering has always been our silent superpower at BVNK. 

Here's a behind-the-scenes look at how we solved some of stablecoin payments' biggest challenges.

Blockchains weren't built for payments at scale. They're brilliant for many things, but they lack many native functions that payment providers need.

Our teams learned this the hard way...
 - scattered liquidity
- unpredictable gas fees
- transactions that would randomly get stuck with zero visibility.

As our volumes grew into the billions, these "minor inconveniences" became million-dollar problems.

So we built something I'm genuinely proud of: automation that handles all the messy blockchain complexities behind the scenes, letting payment operations run smoothly at scale.

It's been hugely impactful for us ($1M saved annually just on gas fee optimization, 100+ hours of operational time recovered each month).

Check out our Engineering Manager Zelun Zhang explaining how we've packaged these hard-won learnings into Layer1 - so other companies don't have to repeat the painful learning curve.

More of these "Scaling Lessons" to come in the next few weeks!

In 1995, a Bulgarian hid $200 in a floppy disk... 

And mailed it to Jeff Bezos' garage.

This became one of Amazon's most legendary orders.

Growing up in Bulgaria, the contrast is wild: 
↳ 2003: My first internet encounter 
Sharing 1 PC with 30 classmates at school

↳ 2007: The first family computer 
A chunky beige box we saved years to buy

Yet in 1995, someone from my country... 
Had already seen what the internet could become.

Their method was brilliantly simple:
↳ Hide cash in a floppy 💾
↳ Label it in English
↳ Bet customs won't understand English

And it worked.

Bezos still tells this story today. 
Not because it was funny. 
Not because it was clever.

But because it proved something bigger: 
People will always find a way.

From hiding cash in floppy disks... 
To one-click ordering and same-day delivery... 
Innovation doesn't wait. 

It just needs someone bold enough to try.

Remember your first internet encounter? 🌐

Training AI Agents without training LLMs

Long before LLMs existed, the AI agents we built were either rule-based or trained with reinforcement learning using POMDPs (Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes). Fast forward to today where AI Agents are tied to the hip with LLMs. However, training LLMs is not always feasible, especially if one is using a state-of-the-art hosted foundation model like GPT or Claude.

Today’s AI agents are heavily reliant on various kinds of memory — short term, long term, episodic — where they store and fetch information to create proper context for the underlying LLM. I have been discussing with friends who worked with me on AI Agents in the past - how well can we train an agent without having to train its LLM? 
For instance, improve task-specific memory storage and retrieval using reinforcement learning?

I was happy to see this paper a couple weeks back and just finished reading it: https://lnkd.in/gdc9Zfew
They show how agents can be fine-tuned by training memory and case-selection policies, while keeping the underlying LLM frozen. The results are strong - better generalization and continual adaptation without expensive model fine-tuning though several open questions remain.

Who else is looking forward to experimenting with this approach for their use case?

The best founders give up.

Not on the mission.
Not on the company.

They give up the right things, at the right time, with the right people.

All of the most successful founders I know give up:
→ The stuff they’re good at but secretly hate
 → The projects that feel “too important” to delegate
 → The zone of competence that’s slowly killing their zone of genius

And I’ve seen what happens when they don’t.
→ Slower decisions
→ Bottlenecked execution
→ Teams trained to wait, not lead

Here’s the rule I live by: You earn the right to give up.

In the early days? Do everything.
Once you raise? Start letting go.

The goal isn’t to stay in the weeds.
It’s to build altitude with clarity.

At Series A, your time is your leverage. 
→ Every hour you spend in a zone of competence is an hour stolen from your zone of genius.

Great founders don’t just scale their company.
→ They scale themselves, by deciding what no longer belongs on their desk.
The faster you can fire yourself from those roles, 
→ the faster your company will scale. 

Do you think “letting go” is delegating, or dumping?

Midjourney generates over $5 million in revenue per employee. Google does about $1.8 million. Meta does about $1.6 million.

Midjourney's founder, David Holz, designed the entire company so that every infrastructure choice was also a headcount choice.

That conviction came from experience. Holz spent twelve years at Leap Motion, where the company raised over $100 million and grew to about 100 people.

It was eventually acquired for $30 million; less than a third of what investors had put in.

Twelve years of building taught him how to build a “minimum viable team” in the early days. A team just big enough to give them a chance at finding product-market fit.

So when he started Midjourney, he launched entirely on Discord instead of building a traditional web app.

This meant: Tiny engineering team. No mobile app maintenance. No authentication system. No support ticketing infrastructure.

Discord provided all of it.

Even better, every image generated in public channels became a free tutorial for newcomers and a free advertisement for the product.

That server now has over 21 million members - the largest community on Discord.

Since then, Midjourney has built far more functionality beyond Discord. But those early constraints are what made the company hyper-efficient when it mattered most.

Software Engineers are becoming "Product Engineers".

If you're only writing code, you're at the risk being replaced by AI. LLMs like Claude 3.7 Sonnet have commoditized coding.

What's truly valuable now are generalists who can code but also have an eye for UI/UX design, a good taste in product, and a deep understanding of the market.

Startups are looking for engineers who talk directly with users and make decisions on what to build, then build with the help of AI.

This approach works because engineers inherently understand both the technical constraints and the opportunities better than anyone else.

The roles of "product manager" and "engineer" will merge. 

This is exactly what we're already doing at Chatbase, everyone acts as their own product manager, with a lot of influence over the design and functionality of features.

This model will soon become the standard for software engineering.

My son made a hat at a birthday party. Loved it. Showed it to everyone. A week later, it was gone. Never mentioned it again.

But his Messi jersey - he wore it four days a week for an entire year. He didn't make it, but it means the world to him.

Both made him happy. They just served different needs.

If you're running a SaaS company right now, you're hearing the noise. "AI will kill SaaS." "Anyone can build software now." "The subscription economy is over."

Vibe coding platforms are generating tens of millions of projects. Some are weekend experiments. Some are experienced engineers shipping in hours what used to take weeks.

The tools are real, the output is accelerating, and it's becoming its own massive category.

But it's a different category from the software your customers reach for every day.

Harvard researchers called it "The IKEA Effect." People who build something themselves value it 63% more than a pre-built version.

That's a real force, and it explains why DIY software will keep growing - for hobbyists and professional builders alike.

It also highlights why the tools people didn't build but still reach for daily earned that place through something else entirely:

- The login that remembered their context.

- The update that didn't break their workflow.

- The edge case you handled before they knew it existed.

Every one of those is a decision your team made well, compounding over years.

Speed of creation is a different advantage than depth of accumulated trust - and your customers feel the difference every time they open your product without thinking twice.

The SaaS market is projected to nearly double by 2030. DIY software will grow into its own massive category. Both things are true, serving different needs.

Hats are fun to make. Jerseys are hard to replace.

Just keep building.

The paper that ignited the current AI revolution, why was it special and where are his authors now?

In 2017 the "Attention Is All You Need" paper was released by Google and took the NLP world by storm. It took many ideas that existed in some ways and combined them into one new Deep Learning architecture. Few special things about it:

1. It was meant to improve results on translation tasks, surprisingly most NLP breakthroughs came from tackling translation tasks.

2. Only a few months after the paper was released it replaced almost all previous approaches for almost any NLP task, this was the fastest research->production adoption at that time (today the pace is even higher)

3. It was one of the only (even the first if we don't count ResNets) papers that invented a completely new architecture for Deep Learning since the 90s

4. To this date this architecture is the foundation of all LLMs with only slight modifications to the core architecture.

And what about the authors? Well, almost all left Google at some point and went on to build companies which raised (to date) over 1.4BN$:

1. Ashish Vaswani and Niki Parmar left in 2021 to found Adept a company that raised over 450M$ to build LLMs that replaces any GUI with Natural Language (interestingly,  both left Adept 5 months ago according to LI) 
2. Noam Shazeer left in 2021 to start Character.AI a company which created characters based chatbots with special personalities, they raised 100M$ recently (and almost 200M$ overall)
3. Aidan Gomez left in 2019 and founded Cohere, one of the 4 main players in the LLMs field which raised over 400M$
4. Illia Polosukhin left in 2017 and founded NEAR Protocol, a web3 startup that raised 375M$ to date
5. Jakob Uszkoreit left in 2021 and founded Inceptive an AI biotech company which raised 20M$ to date
6. Lukasz Kaiser left in 2021 and joined OpenAI
7. And finally Llion Jones is the only one that still works at Google

As you can see, this paper was no accident, it was a seminal work done by a group of superstars, kudos to them, and amazing times for us.

ChatGPT's first brand campaign is a bet on everyday humanity.

Earlier this year, OpenAI used the Super Bowl to position ChatGPT as one of humanity’s greatest inventions. That was a launch moment. 

Now comes the harder part… sustaining a brand story.

The new campaign trades spectacle for intimacy. Shot on 35mm film, it shows quiet, everyday scenes. A date night dinner, a sibling road trip, a new workout routine. 

Each unfolds naturally until a prompt appears on screen, revealing the invisible assist that shaped it. ChatGPT’s replies scroll upward like movie credits, framing the product as a co-creator rather than the star.

It’s an intentional choice to ground AI in the human day-to-day. 

Because the real cultural tension around AI isn’t capability. It’s emotion. Will this technology flatten creativity and connection, or will it help people feel more of both? 

By anchoring the campaign in craft and showing ChatGPT as a quiet helper, OpenAI is betting on the latter.

There’s also a category play here. ChatGPT has ~700M weekly users. Anthropic’s Claude sits at ~20–30M monthly. With that scale, OpenAI doesn’t need to shout about features.

It needs to lower the barrier for the AI-curious, the people who still wonder whether this tool belongs in their lives. 

Everyday wins are the bridge.

If you zoom out, it’s a smart contrast with Anthropic’s recent “AI for problem solvers” push.

→ Claude leans into rigor, reliability, and complex problem solving. 

→ OpenAI leans into warmth, accessibility, and habit formation. Both lanes matter, but only one can claim the cultural center of gravity.

OpenAI’s human-first or Anthropic’s problem-solver… which style wins your attention?

I spend wayyy too much time on this platform. My agency, Compound, ships 150+ pieces of content every week for our clients. Here are some field notes on what I see (actually) working on LinkedIn for b2b execs going into May ‘26.

(1) “Just post 5x per week” is dead advice. Yeah. You should be consistent. But if you cannot tie every post you publish back to a content pillar, you’ll end up spinning your wheels. Every post needs a real WHY, and a post-mortem theory for why it worked or didn't.

(2) You must ‘pair every take with a story.’ Even this post—a listicle of LinkedIn advice—is framed as a ‘story’ (field notes from my lived experience) rather than some lame ‘5 Tips To Grow Your Exec’s LinkedIn Presence 🤓’ listicle. Framing matters.

(3) Your data is your moat. Some of the most effective companies on LinkedIn (see Ramp, Carta, etc) do an exceptional job of sharing insights from their own data. Marketing leaders get hung up on this because they think this type of content must be a huge “State of [INDUSTRY]” report. It can be, but doesn’t have to. Start simple with a unique, counterintuitive insight from your customer data (that you have approval to share).

(4) Build a Content Ecosystem. Having one exec active on LinkedIn is table stakes. Winning companies are layering in multiple executive accounts. The most basic version of this is having your CEO and CTO both post. But really, try to get to a point where you have 3-5+ execs posting ~3-5x per week on LinkedIn.

(5) Treat your office like a set. Posts with relevant media tend to outperform. In-person companies have a competitive advantage in that their office provides endless opportunities for photos and B-roll footage. Take advantage of this.

(6) Beware the Top of Funnel Trap. It’s quite easy to ‘go viral’ on LinkedIn by posting generic business-adjacent slop advice or trying too hard to be funny or—as the kids say—‘unhinged.’ Some b2b companies can nail humor. Most shouldn’t (coming from someone who was once meme-pilled). There are ways to inject personality and humor into your content without becoming the court jester of LinkedIn.

Running out of room here, so might end up doing a Part II or turning this into a longer-form newsletter. If that’d be helpful, let me know.

In the meantime, share this post with your CEO who’s been trying to ‘grow on LinkedIn’ this year.

Look for time sinks, spreadsheets, and hacked-together workflows that people already pay to solve.
Don't try to invent smth never seen before if this is your first startup. You're either a genius or it's not going to work, and it's most likely the latter.

2. 𝗦𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗮𝗻 𝗠𝗩𝗣 𝗶𝗻 3 𝗱𝗮𝘆𝘀.
Your only goal here is to have a Stripe button on a landing page. Anything more is just procrastination.

3. 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝗽𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗳𝗿𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗶𝘁 𝗮𝘀 𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴, 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗻𝗴.
Talk like a friend showing progress, not a founder pitching. 

4. 𝗠𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝘀𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗲𝘅𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗳𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝘄𝗼𝗿𝗸 𝗳𝗹𝗮𝘄𝗹𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗹𝘆 𝗯𝗲𝗳𝗼𝗿𝗲 𝗮𝗱𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗻𝗲𝘄 𝗳𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀.
This will reduce churn of your users and increase long term trust. Your MVP should be very small and very reliable.

5. 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝘂𝗮𝗹𝗹𝘆 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗳𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 100 𝗰𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗿𝘀.
DM people in niche communities who've complained about the exact problem you solve.
Create value-first posts: "Built this tool that [solves X problem], looking for 5 testers..."

6. 𝗠𝗶𝗻𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘇𝗲 𝗰𝗹𝗶𝗰𝗸𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗲 “𝗮𝗵𝗮” 𝗺𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁.
 Every extra click is a tax on conversion. Simplify the path from signup → value.

7. 𝗚𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗮𝗺𝗮𝘇𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗿 𝘀𝘂𝗽𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁.
Users willing to talk are basically paying to be your focus group. Treat them well.

8. 𝗦𝗼𝗺𝗲𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗯𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵𝘁? 𝗧𝗮𝗹𝗸 𝘁𝗼 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗺 (𝗮 𝗹𝗼𝘁).
Jump on calls, watch them screen‑share, ask why they almost didn’t buy.

9. 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿 𝘃𝗶𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗳𝗲𝗲𝗱𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸 𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗽𝘀.
Partner with the influencers other influencers copy. 
Talk about your growth for more growth.

10. 𝗦𝗘𝗢 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗯𝗲𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗳𝘂𝗹, 𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴.
Blog today so Google sends users tomorrow, next month, and next year.

If your product disappeared tomorrow, how many people would be genuinely annoyed?

That question is a better PMF test than "signups" or "followers."

It comes from Kevin Kelly's "1,000 True Fans" essay. The math: 1,000 people paying $100/year = $100K.

Creators quote this constantly. Founders rarely do.

Kelly's definition of a true fan is deliberately extreme: someone who will reliably buy what you make because they're buying you, not a particular SKU.

You don't wake up to product-market fit. You assemble it. One customer conversation a day. One onboarding fix. One reason for someone to stay instead of churn.

A mass audience tempts you to optimize for what gets attention at scale. A true-fan audience forces you toward value, which compounds more slowly but doesn't evaporate.

If you want a customer base built on word of mouth, you have the obligation to be worth sharing.

That obligation is a better compass than likes.

What would it take for 1,000 people to genuinely miss what you're building?

One day you're eating a chocolate bar.

Next thing you know, you're cheating on your wife.

Imam Ghazali said the stomach is the root of all desire.

It starts with gluttony. It never stops at food.

When you give in to that craving, it's not about the dessert. 

It's the act of indulging. 

You're training yourself to surrender to whatever you want, whenever you want it.

And hunger isn't just your stomach.

It's every desire you have.

You give in to food. Then you give in to sleep. Then laziness. Then your gaze. 

Then something you'd never admit out loud.

Each "just this once" weakens the muscle. One door opens the next.

That's the truth Ghazali was teaching us.

The man who controls his stomach controls his life. The man who doesn't is a slave to every desire that comes knocking.

Discipline isn't built in the gym. It's not built in the masjid.

It's built alone. At the kitchen counter. When no one's watching.

That's where the real jihad is.

Abraham Maslow's famous hierarchy of needs has a hidden origin story.

And it challenges everything we think we know about human motivation.

In 1938, before publishing his groundbreaking theory, Maslow spent six weeks living with the Blackfoot Nation in Alberta, Canada. 

What he discovered there transformed his understanding of human potential forever.

Here's what shocked him:

1/ Cultural Wealth Paradigm
↳ 80-90% of Blackfoot members possessed self-esteem qualities found in only 5-10% of his own population
↳ Wealth meant giving possessions away, not accumulating them
↳ Community status came from generosity, not individual achievement

2/ The Giveaway Ceremony
↳ Maslow witnessed community members publicly give away all their possessions
↳ Those with the most gave the most to those in greatest need
↳ Self-actualization wasn't earned—it was the foundation of community life

3/ Inverted Worldview
↳ Blackfoot philosophy places self-actualization at the BASE, not the top
↳ Individual fulfillment supports community actualization
↳ People arrive "already actualized"—community helps them stay that way

The Blackfoot didn't see human needs as a ladder to climb.

They saw them as a tipi reaching toward the sky. Self-actualization was the foundation supporting everything above.

While scholars debate whether Maslow directly appropriated Blackfoot knowledge, one thing is clear: 

Indigenous communities had sophisticated frameworks for human development centuries before Western psychology existed.

Yet Maslow never acknowledged this influence in his 1943 paper.

Today, Indigenous scholars like Dr. Cindy Blackstock are reclaiming these teachings, developing models that prioritize community well-being over individual achievement. Modern research increasingly validates these approaches.

The question isn't whether Maslow "stole" the hierarchy.

It's why we've ignored Indigenous wisdom for so long.
===========================
⁉️ Did you know about the link between Maslow and the Blackfoot Nation?
♻️ Share to educate your network about this little-acknowledged link.

Character inconsistency is over. Ideogram launched Character Reference - the AI filmmaker’s casting director.

When creating video's with AI, you don't just design images.

You cast characters.

Not just frontal portraits. But side angles, low angles, emotional close-ups, wide shots - across scenes, styles and moments.

And up until now, getting one character to show up consistently has been..frustrating. 

Sure, Midjourney's OmniReference and Flux's Kontext Pro Max made great progress. But I think Ideogram just set a new standard. 

How it works: 
- Snap a photo with your camera
- Use an image you generated inside Ideogram
- Or upload one from anywhere else.

Then just describe the shot. 

Camera angle, lighting, costume, emotion. 
Ideogram will generate 4 consistent frames. 

Need a different outfit or wider angle? 
Just iterate, your character stays locked in. 

No more 'close enough'.

You get your lead actor every. single. time. 

It also integrates with other Ideogram features:

- Remix (update characters inside a specific shot)
- Magic Fill (swap characters into an existing frame)
- Masking tools (keep accessories or hairstyles consistent).

I'm an avid Ideogram fan since day one so it's amazing to see them raise the bar like this. 

Not just a feature. 
A new standard. 

If you're building a film, brand or storyverse with recurring characters, you got to try it.

Today, we’re announcing Vanta's Series D and new valuation: $4.15 billion.

Vanta’s mission is to help businesses earn and prove trust. 

We started by automating SOC 2 for startups. Our first product helped startups compete on a level playing field and build trust faster. Before Vanta, “you can’t automate a SOC 2.”

Then, as we’d hoped, the world began to move past annual, check-the-box audits.

The bar to demonstrate trust ratcheted up, and we responded by building Trust Centers and products for vendor risk, access reviews, and risk management. We want to shift security reviews from static, point-in-time checks to continuous, automated verification.

I’m a fan of the parable about “the back of the chessboard,” which explains the power of compounding. If Vanta’s taught me anything, it’s to be in awe of compounding. It feels like we’re stacking the chessboard at Vanta: 

* 200M+ assets continuously monitored from laptops to servers to employees
* 1.5M employees and 33,000 vendors managed to reduce access risk and secure third-party relationships
* 1.7B vulnerabilities remediated within SLA, keeping customers ahead
* 10M Trust Center views, each one a potential purchase made faster with automated, self-serve security reviews

Vanta is now critical infrastructure to build and prove business-to-business trust, and we see it in our customers: Atlassian, whose 400+ partners showcase trust via compliance milestones and Trust Centers. Or Ramp, which eliminated spreadsheets by mapping custom controls into continuously-monitored frameworks, saving time and money. Cursor gains real-time visibility into the security posture to maintain compliance confidently as they scale. WRITER completes vendor reviews and access audits in under 20 minutes with Vanta AI. Clay credits Vanta with saving “hundreds of hours and well over six figures” in potential lost deals or added headcount. The list goes on.

Financing milestones are days of fanfare (please like, share, follow, etc.) and they’re just markers on a longer journey. For every heaving middle of the chessboard, there are small and unobtrusive beginnings: one grain of rice, two grains of rice, “a brutalist white website," no one notices.

To our customers and partners: thank you. You’ve pushed us to move faster, go deeper, and focus on what matters. Your feedback – good, bad, in between – has helped improve Vanta and make us better.

To the Vanta team past and present: thank you for building the future of trust with care, creativity, and grit. Thank you for trusting us with your time and your careers! It remains the privilege of my career to hold a microphone to your work.

$50 million in recruiter earnings - Paraform's building a new category of how recruiters work.

Weddings paid for. Mortgages covered. Student loans fully repaid.

This is a huge milestone for us.
 
When Jeffrey and I first started Paraform, we knew companies needed help scaling, but couldn't figure out how to consistently get the right candidates in front of them. We started by experimenting with a peer referral model, but it fell flat.
 
Then recruiters started showing up in our workflow, completely changing the trajectory of Paraform.
 
What we didn't understand at first was just how powerful a great recruiter's network is. Their networks are warm, and they've spent years building deep relationships. And they're super consistent because it's their profession. 
 
We realized this model was scalable. One peer referral gets you one qualified candidate. One recruiter can surface hundreds.
 
2 years ago, we went all in and gave professional recruiters the infrastructure to do what they do best - but with better tools, better access to clients/searches, and more freedom.
 
$50M in recruiter earnings later, they're winning more than ever.
 
And I'm grateful for what that's meant for recruiters' lives. We've seen weddings paid for, mortgages covered, and student loans cleared.
 
The momentum here is only compounding. We paid out a record $6M to recruiters in February alone and are already on track to blow that number out of the water next month.

There's one thing that most founders forget when pitching to investors. It's this:


You are the pitch. 

Your unique skill set. Your life experiences. Your network. Everything that makes you unique and has led you to dive head first into launching a startup. 

The product will change. 
The business model will change.
The go-to-market strategy will change. 

But the core of the business will always be you as a founder. As you grow, the team you assemble around you becomes just as important as you. 

Investors want to understand you and who you are. 

Sometimes we feel uncomfortable talking about ourselves, but as an investor, when it comes to answering the question 'Do I back this person', the intricate details of your business won't matter; the default will always be what makes this founder truly exceptional. 

Tell your story, share your energy and passion, but most importantly of all, never downplay what makes you unique.

Feeling “stuck” in life or work has nothing to do with how smart you are.

Kevin Kelly (Wired co-founder) published 460 bits of life advice in his book. The problem with his advice isn't quality - it's that any good advice is easy to nod at and surprisingly hard to live.

Here are 5 that convert intention into motion:

1. The important is rarely urgent. The urgent is rarely important.

The urgent stuff is loud: Slack threads, calendar invites, customer escalations. You can finish it in 12 minutes and feel productive.

The important stuff is quiet: the product decision that removes a class of support tickets six months from now, the positioning rewrite that makes your sales cycle shorter, the hard conversation you keep postponing.

Practical move: if something is genuinely urgent, it will survive a short delay. If it can't, it just wanted attention.

2. When you're stuck, shrink the task until you can move.

The brain doesn't start big things. It starts small things, then accidentally continues.

Kelly's version is almost comically practical: find the smallest possible action that moves the project forward. So small it feels slightly stupid.

Not "fix onboarding." Watch one user fail in the first 30 seconds.

When we weren't seeing true word-of-mouth growth at Gamma, the fix wasn't a dozen initiatives. It was obsessing over one narrow slice - the first moments of the experience - until it worked.

3. More people fail to start than fail to finish.

Starting is hard because it creates evidence. Before you start, you can still believe you "could totally do it." Once you start, reality begins keeping score.

4. The work you should be doing has "enjoyable tedium."

A good sign you're doing the right kind of work is that you enjoy the tedious parts other people find torturous.

Companies are mostly built in the tedious parts: the fifteenth onboarding iteration, the nth customer call, the roadmap tradeoff that no one will ever praise you for.

Enjoyable tedium is one of the few durable signals that you're in a domain where compounding is possible.

5. For difficult things, surround yourself with friends.

Friends here means people who can keep you in the work when your mind tries to negotiate you out of it.

This can look like a weekly standing session where you show someone what you shipped (or didn't).

If you're feeling stuck, this is often the fastest unblock. Not because your friends will solve the problem, but because they'll change your emotional posture from "I'm alone with this" to "we're in it."

The hardest part isn't knowing what to do. It's surviving the moment when reality starts keeping score.

Travis Kalanick came out of stealth on Friday after 8 years of silence.

Imagine this:

You build Uber from $0 to $70 billion and change how people move across every city.

Then in 2017, investors hand you a letter demanding you resign.

So you step down. Leave heartbroken.

The board replaces you and new leadership sells Uber’s self-driving unit, literally the thing you believed was Uber’s entire future.

Now gone.

Sold for $4 billion to Aurora.

The media writes your obituary. Toxic culture and another cautionary tale.

The tech ecosystem moves on. But you don’t.

--

You go quiet and take the next big bet. You buy a ghost kitchen company called CloudKitchens for $150 million. It ends up raising over $1 billion.

CloudKitchens hits a $15 billion valuation by 2022 with thousands of employees.

But this is just the start. Most people think it’s just one company focused on kitchens.

In reality, there’s CloudKitchens for ghost kitchens, Otter for restaurant tech (rolled out across 100k+ restaurants), and Lab37 for robotics R&D.

You rename the parent company Atoms.

A robotics company.

1. Food
2. Mining
3. Transport

The first step is acquiring Pronto, the autonomous vehicle startup built by Anthony Levandowski, the same engineer recruited from Google to build Uber’s self-driving program.

Levandowski deployed 100+ autonomous trucks for Heidelberg Materials, one of the world’s largest building materials companies.

Now he’s coming back. Reports say Uber itself is backing you to go after self-driving again. The company that pushed you out may now be funding your return.

Your manifesto ends with this:

“When I told my friends, family and colleagues about my plans for what was next, they were really excited that I was ‘coming back’. The thing is, I never left.”

--

The bad guys took his company.
Never broke his spirit.
He rebuilt from scratch.
And now he’s back.

I learned this week that McKinsey & Company pays tens of thousands of dollars to the best technical experts and industry operators to show up at high-stakes client pitches and win work. $20,000, even $30,000 per hour, isn’t unusual. They’ve turned sending the right people to the right meetings into an art form. Credibility is the strategy.

The reality is, the traditional Consulting profile no longer cuts it. Clients are too sophisticated, they can smell the BS from a mile away. The days of polished consultants reciting frameworks and buzzwords are over. Ask a few specific operational questions, and many crumble. Clients want to hear from people who have actually led transformations, failed, fixed it, and built something that works.

Across every major Consulting firm we work with, the hiring pattern is clear: blended teams. Technical depth and lived experience now matter more than polish and pedigree. Firms are scouring the market for people who are truly tech-savvy and who’ve sat in the client’s seat, people who understand how messy, political, and hard execution really is. They’ll pay a premium for that.

Teams full of lifetime MBAs and consultants will die fast. There’s a lost generation being replaced by AI on one side and real operators on the other: grizzled execs and geeks who can ship product, code, or strategy that actually lands.

The future belongs to those who can bridge both worlds: analytical precision and operational scars. Everyone else will be replaced by algorithms, or ignored by clients.

Kook-Surf taught me the most expensive lesson in startups:

Pick battles where your advantages compound, not where external forces decide your speed.

We just hit $100M ARR at Clay. It took us six years to go from $0-1m, then two years to go from $1-100m. I’m going to walk you through the 6 biggest GTM bets that got us here.

$100M ARR may be the headline, but I’m most proud of how we accomplished it: we’ve never churned an enterprise customer, have >200% enterprise NRR, every dollar we invest grows 15x, and we’ve created a culture of creativity and belonging (with a perfect Glassdoor score to match!).

Note:
-We are a product-driven company. Without that foundation and a unique POV, none of this would work.
-Our GTM approach is authentic to us. Greatness comes from doing what only you can do.

1. Building a self-serve motion through reverse demos

We originally had a product that nobody could use. It took us 8 calls to sell a $200/mo product! Reverse demos were key to bringing that to zero. Customers would share their screen, and we’d use Zoom annotations to solve their problem in 30mins. They accomplished something real, learned how to use Clay, and we got so much UI feedback.

2. An irrational investment in brand

Most B2B startups treat brand as a post-PMF investment. We flipped that. We bought Clay(.)com and hired a claymation artist before we had revenue. Our Head of Brand was employee #18. These choices felt irrational but they’re authentic to us. Now it’s a moat. 

3. Switching to usage-based pricing

We were the first GTM company to offer usage-based pricing. Our customers were shocked we didn't charge per seat. But we're built for efficiency. Usage-based pricing helped us target more technical users and enabled our land-and-expand motion. 

4. Building an agency motion to generate UGC on LinkedIn

Cold email agencies were our first customers. They posted about Clay organically to position themselves as experts and win clients. We pounced on it and enabled them. This sparked a self-perpetuating cycle: new people discover Clay through that content, join, create their own, and earn recognition too.

5. Unconventional hiring

50% of our business teams are doing their job for the first time. This is how we bring creativity into our company and think differently. We’ve hired farmers, archaeologists, magicians in new roles. We look for product passion, customer empathy and technical curiosity, then teach the mechanics.

6. We created a new career path & economy: GTM Engineering

There are now thousands of open GTME jobs and hundreds of agencies built around it. Many first-time entrepreneurs have already built 7-figure businesses on top of Clay. Our community, with clubs in more than 70 cities, is our force multiplier, and it’s the clearest sign that we’re building something meaningful. 

-
All of these bets show we’re not racing anyone. We spent six years figuring out what and how we wanted to build. In an era of overnight successes and growth at all costs, it turns out that taking time to build something authentic can create a business with bigger impact & more growth than you'd think.

Will your cofounder leave? Definitely not 😇

But for those other founding teams...it's a real risk. Although perhaps the rate of cofounder breakups is declining slightly!

Here's the data:

We looked at 18,920 startups that founded their companies between 2016-2025.

These companies had to have all the below:

a) been started in the US
b) had 2 founders at the start
b) raised at least some VC or angel cash (SAFEs or Notes count)

Each founding year vintage moves left to right—for example, in the 2017 cohort, 25.6% of companies had lost a cofounder by year 4.

𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘀 𝗧𝗼 𝗡𝗼𝘁𝗲

• Cofounders left more often as the years progressed.

• But the 2020-2022 period was the peak. Probably due to many factors (overvaluations, interest rate changes, startup funding flooded then sharply pulled, etc). 

• Typically founders have a 4-year vest on their shares. That means that about a quarter of founders will leave before their vest is complete (although it may be a higher if the vest didn't begin until the first VC round).

Every founder should have a vesting schedule on their equity. Absolute must-have. But the question of vesting schedule length has come up more frequently of late.

Should founder vesting start at 6 years given how long building a VC-backed business takes these days? Or should founders stick to the traditional 4-year vest?

No surprise that VCs would argue for the former but I think it can be a reasonable position. Founders can equally reasonably push back by saying if the VCs want us vested longer that should be a negotiating point during the deal. And of course there needs to be some way to acknowledge the actual dollars that many founders pour into their companies in the early days. 

All of which brings us right back to the most crucial question in your company: who are you building it with?

PS: way more data on this cofounder dynamic and other questions in our Founder Ownership 2026 report - live now: https://lnkd.in/grPj33p7 🙏
